title: The 100k Whys of AI
blurb: The author argues that AI-generated text is distinguishable from human writing not because LLMs have alien mannerisms, but because they fall back on the same complex set of mannerisms in response to almost any prompt. The evidence is a collage of roughly 150 near-identical Amazon book covers surfaced by searching "100000 whys," many featuring the same roaring dinosaur, rocket, or golden retriever. Because the tools are quasi-deterministic, a hundred authors giving similar prompts get functionally identical output around 80% of the time.

title: Nobel laureate John Jumper is leaving DeepMind for rival Anthropic
blurb: Nobel laureate John Jumper is leaving Google DeepMind for Anthropic after nearly nine years leading the AlphaFold team. His exit follows Character AI co-founder Noam Shazeer's departure from DeepMind to OpenAI this week, marking back-to-back losses of senior researchers to rival labs.

title: Prompt Injection as Role Confusion
blurb: This post argues that prompt injection stems from a fundamental flaw in how LLMs perceive roles, since everything a model receives, including system prompts, user messages, tool outputs, and its own reasoning, arrives as one continuous token stream partitioned only by role tags. From this framing the authors construct new attacks, explain existing mechanistic interpretability results, and predict when injections succeed, then sketch a research agenda for a science of roles.

title: How we built an internal data analytics agent
blurb: GitHub built Qubot, an internal Copilot-powered analytics agent that lets any employee query the company's data warehouse in plain language and get answers within seconds. Accessible through Slack, VS Code, and the Copilot CLI, it spawns a Copilot Cloud Agent per question, targets exploratory analysis rather than dashboards, and stores each result as a markdown report in a pull request for reuse.

title: Anthropic’s powerful Mythos AI reportedly breached ‘almost all’ NSA classified systems within a few hours during red-team test — report sheds more light on the U.S. government's sudden ban on the flagship models
blurb: The Economist reported that Anthropic's Mythos model breached "almost all" of the NSA's classified systems within hours during an authorized red-team test, according to NSA director Gen. Joshua Rudd. The disclosure explains the June 12 US directive barring foreign nationals from accessing the Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, which prompted Anthropic to disable both globally rather than enforce nationality-based access.

title: Lighthouse agentic browsing scoring
blurb: Lighthouse added an Agentic Browsing category that runs deterministic audits on how well a site supports machine interaction, checking WebMCP tool registration via the Chrome DevTools Protocol and a filtered subset of accessibility-tree signals like programmatic names, role integrity, and visibility. Rather than a 0-100 score, it reports a fractional pass ratio plus pass/fail status, since standards for the agentic web are still emerging.

title: OpenAI expands Daybreak with Patch the Planet and full GPT-5.5-Cyber release
blurb: OpenAI expanded its Daybreak cybersecurity program with Patch the Planet, an open-source patching initiative built with Trail of Bits and HackerOne, alongside an updated Codex Security plugin and the full release of GPT-5.5-Cyber, its most capable defensive model. More than 30 projects including cURL, Python, and the Go project have committed to join, with a human security engineer reviewing every finding before it reaches a maintainer.

title: Qualcomm Nears Deal for AI Chip Startup Modular
blurb: Qualcomm is in advanced talks to acquire AI chip startup Modular in a deal valuing the company at roughly $4B. The acquisition would expand Qualcomm's data center ambitions, adding Modular's software stack for running AI workloads across different hardware as the company pushes beyond its mobile chip roots.

title: AWS enters the context layer race with a graph that learns from agents, not manual curation
blurb: AWS announced a context intelligence stack for AI agents, centered on AWS Context, a knowledge graph service that automatically maps relationships across existing data and refines itself based on how agents query it. The launch also includes general availability of Amazon S3 Annotations and a preview of skill assets in AWS Glue Data Catalog, with all metadata published in Apache Iceberg format and queries inheriting IAM and Lake Formation permissions.

title: Sources: Qualcomm is in advanced talks to acquire Modular in a transaction valuing the AI chip startup at ~$4B, up from a $1.6B valuation in September 2025 (Bloomberg)
blurb: Qualcomm is in advanced talks to acquire AI chip startup Modular in a deal valuing it at roughly $4B, according to Bloomberg. The figure marks a sharp jump from Modular's $1.6B valuation in September 2025, signaling Qualcomm's push to expand its position in AI compute.

title: Alphabet shares close down 5% on Monday following the departure of Google DeepMind VP John Jumper, the company's second top AI executive to leave in a week (Bloomberg)
blurb: Alphabet shares closed down 5% on Monday after Google DeepMind VP John Jumper departed, the second senior AI executive to leave the company in a week. Jumper shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for his work on AlphaFold, and his exit adds to investor concern over talent retention as rivals aggressively recruit top AI researchers.

title: Nvidia Halos
blurb: NVIDIA introduced Halos, a full-stack safety system that unifies safety across vehicle architecture, AI models, chips, software, and tools for developing and deploying autonomous vehicles from cloud to car. The system spans design-time, deployment-time, and validation-time guardrails running on DGX for training, Omniverse and Cosmos for simulation, and DRIVE AGX for deployment, and is now extending beyond AVs to robotics.

title: Microsoft and Chevron plan one of the largest gas-powered data center projects in US
blurb: Microsoft and Chevron announced plans to develop Project Kilby, a 2.67-gigawatt natural gas plant in West Texas dedicated to powering a Microsoft AI and cloud data center. The 20-year power purchase agreement marks a shift for a company pledged to eliminate its carbon emissions by 2030, with the plant projected to release over 13 million tons of carbon dioxide annually.

title: Red-Teaming after Mythos — Zico Kolter & Matt Fredrikson, Gray Swan
blurb: Gray Swan cofounders Zico Kolter and Matt Fredrikson argue that AI security is a distinct discipline from traditional cybersecurity, because agents like Codex and Claude Code introduce a new exploit class through indirect prompt injection. They detail their red-teaming stack, including Shade, a tool Anthropic used to test model robustness, and explain why frontier models do not get automatically safer as they scale and why specialized red-teaming models can now beat humans at breaking AI systems.

title: Groq Raises $650 Million to Aid in Pivot After Nvidia Deal
blurb: Groq raised $650 million in a new funding round to expand its data center capacity and reposition itself as an AI computing provider. The round follows Groq's deal with Nvidia and accelerates the chip startup's pivot from selling its own inference hardware toward offering broader compute capacity to customers.

title: Air Space Intelligence won an $875M, 12-year FAA contract to develop AI tools that map flight trajectories and identify areas of congestion to reduce delays (Allyson Versprille/Bloomberg)
blurb: Air Space Intelligence won an $875M, 12-year contract with the FAA to build AI tools that map flight trajectories and flag congested airspace to reduce delays. The systems are intended to give controllers better routing intelligence across the national airspace, where chronic congestion and staffing shortages have driven persistent delays.

title: SpaceX to acquire the AI coding startup Cursor for $60 billion
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire AI coding startup Cursor for $60B in stock, a deal expected to close in Q3 pending regulatory approval. The acquisition is meant to bolster SpaceX's frontier AI efforts against Anthropic and OpenAI after its xAI merger, though Cursor's category share has slipped from 41% in June 2025 to about 26% in May.

title: Anthropic ships major Claude Design overhaul with design system imports, code round-trips, and a fix for its token-burning problem
blurb: Anthropic shipped a major overhaul of Claude Design that adds design system imports from GitHub repos and design files, code round-trips, and a fix for the tool's heavy token consumption. Once a design system is imported, Claude builds with those components, checks its output against the system, and auto-corrects to keep generated work on brand and connected to existing enterprise tools.

title: Amazon hopes to challenge Nvidia more directly by selling its AI chips
blurb: Amazon's AI chief Peter DeSantis told Bloomberg that AWS is in early talks to sell its Trainium AI chips to other companies for use in their own data centers, marking a more direct challenge to Nvidia. CEO Andy Jassy has said the chip unit would run at roughly $50B annually as a standalone business, against Nvidia's $326B revenue run rate.

title: Dell launches AI server based on Nvidia Vera Rubin GPUs
blurb: Dell launched the PowerEdge XE8812, a liquid-cooled AI server built on Nvidia's Vera Rubin NVL4 architecture that scales to 144 GPUs per rack as the centerpiece of its Dell AI Factory with Nvidia platform. The system expands from 144 to 176 cores and adds 50% more memory per socket and GPU over the prior GB200 generation, letting HPC users run larger models entirely in-memory.

title: SpaceX signs a computing deal worth up to $6.3B with Reflection AI for access to Nvidia GB300s at Colossus 2; Reflection will pay $150M per month through 2029 (Deirdre Bosa/CNBC)
blurb: SpaceX signed a computing deal worth up to $6.3B with Reflection AI, giving the startup access to Nvidia GB300s at the Colossus 2 data center. Reflection will pay $150M per month through 2029 under the agreement, marking another large compute commitment as AI labs lock in long-term GPU capacity.

title: Arm servers capture over 45% of data center market revenue — GPU clusters and high-end AI infrastructure fuel a tectonic shift away from x86
blurb: Arm-based servers now command over 45% of data center revenue, according to IDC, as GPU- and ASIC/FPGA-accelerated systems generated more than 70% of global server revenue in Q1 2026. The overall server market hit a record $122.6B for the quarter, up 30.4% year-over-year, with Dell leading at $20.3B in revenue after AI demand drove 244.1% growth.

title: Zero-Touch OAuth for MCP
blurb: The Enterprise-Managed Authorization (EMA) extension for the Model Context Protocol is now stable, letting organizations manage MCP server access centrally through their existing identity provider. Instead of per-app OAuth, the client obtains an Identity Assertion JWT Authorization Grant during single sign-on and exchanges it for an access token, giving users zero-touch setup and security teams central policy and audit control. Anthropic, Microsoft, and Okta are among early adopters.

title: Trump says he no longer views Anthropic as a national security threat after G7 meeting with CEO
blurb: Trump said in a pretaped Axios interview that he no longer considers Anthropic a national security threat, reversing the administration's stance after meeting CEO Dario Amodei at the G7 Summit. The shift follows a June 12 Commerce Department directive requiring US approval before foreign nationals access Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models, restrictions Trump said he would consider easing.

title: Google DeepMind bets $75M on AI’s future in Hollywood with A24 deal
blurb: Google DeepMind invested $75M in indie film studio A24 to jointly develop AI tools for filmmaking, in what both companies billed as a first-of-its-kind partnership. The deal gives DeepMind direct feedback from A24's filmmakers and follows similar moves by Netflix, which acquired AI tooling firm InterPositive, and Amazon's MGM Studios.

title: CCCL Runtime: A Modern C++ Runtime for CUDA
blurb: NVIDIA introduced CCCL runtime, a set of idiomatic modern C++ APIs that reimplement core CUDA functionality including stream management, memory allocation, and kernel launches through headers like `<cuda/stream>`, `<cuda/buffer>`, and `<cuda/launch>`. It serves as an alternative to the traditional CUDA runtime API, using modern C++ features for safer, composable abstractions while shipping compatibility helpers that let developers adopt it incrementally without rewriting surrounding code.

title: AI-Services Startup Baseten Nabs $13 Billion Valuation
blurb: Baseten raised $1.5B in a Series F at a $13B valuation, co-led by Altimeter, to expand its infrastructure for running lower-cost AI models. The company sells software and compute capacity to firms deploying open and cheaper models, positioning it against larger cloud providers as demand for inference shifts toward cost efficiency.

title: AI chipmaker Groq confirms $650M raise, re-staffs after Nvidia’s $20B not-acqui-hire deal
blurb: Groq confirmed a $650M funding round led by Disruptive and Infinitum, roughly six months after Nvidia licensed its LPU technology non-exclusively and hired away founder Jonathan Ross and other executives. The chipmaker, last valued at $6.9B, has pivoted to its neocloud business, now spanning 13 data centers and serving over five million developers while processing trillions of tokens weekly.

title: Sakana AI releases Fugu Ultra system to rival top AI labs
blurb: Sakana AI released Fugu Ultra, a frontier orchestration model available worldwide through a single OpenAI-compatible API that it positions against Anthropic's Fable 5 and Mythos Preview. Rather than running as a single model, Fugu Ultra delegates subtasks to a pool of expert LLMs, including instances of itself, and matches or surpasses competitors on coding, reasoning, and scientific benchmarks.

title: Filing: Oracle's global workforce fell by 21,000 employees in the past 12 months to 141,000 as of May 31; the company says AI adoption has led to reductions (Brody Ford/Bloomberg)
blurb: Oracle's global workforce shrank by 21,000 employees over the past 12 months to 141,000 as of May 31, according to a regulatory filing. The company attributes the reductions to AI adoption, marking one of the clearest links a major enterprise software vendor has drawn between AI deployment and headcount cuts.

title: AI Coding Agent Horror Stories: The 13-Hour AWS Outage
blurb: This Docker post recounts a coding-agent failure in which an AI agent's unchecked actions triggered a 13-hour AWS outage. It uses the incident to argue for stronger guardrails around autonomous agents, including sandboxed execution, scoped permissions, and human review before agents touch production infrastructure.

title: No Claude Fable 5? No problem: Sakana achieves frontier performance with new Fugu multi-model, auto synthesis system
blurb: Sakana launched Fugu, a multi-agent orchestration system that exposes a single OpenAI-compatible API and dynamically routes queries across a swappable pool of specialized models. CEO David Ha positioned it as a hedge against vendor lock-in after Anthropic revoked public access to Claude Mythos 5 and Fable 5 under a U.S. export control order, claiming Fugu matches those restricted models on third-party agentic benchmarks.

title: Inference chip startup Groq raises $650M to grow its cloud platform
blurb: Groq raised $650M to expand its LPU-powered inference cloud, in a round led by Disruptive and Infinitum that follows its $20B chip licensing deal with Nvidia. The company said its platform now processes trillions of tokens per week for 5 million developers across 13 data centers, and it will use the proceeds to reach 200 megawatts of inference capacity by 2027.

title: Unsloth GLM-5.2 – How to Run Locally
blurb: Unsloth released Dynamic GGUF quantizations of Z.ai's GLM-5.2, a 744B-parameter mixture-of-experts model with 40B active parameters and a 1M-token context window that can run locally. The 2-bit quant fits in 245GB of total memory and retains about 82% accuracy while being 84% smaller, letting the model run on a 256GB unified-memory Mac or a single 24GB GPU with RAM offloading.

title: Nvidia backs AI networking switch silicon startup Upscale in $190M raise
blurb: Upscale AI raised $190M in a Series A-1 round led by Premji Invest, with Nvidia joining the extension round alongside Salesforce Ventures and Temasek. The funding brings the AI networking silicon startup to $500M in total funding at a $2B valuation, supporting its open-standard SkyHammer switch chips built for large-scale, synchronized AI workloads.

title: Patch the Planet: a Daybreak initiative to support open source maintainers
blurb: OpenAI introduced Patch the Planet, a Daybreak initiative built with Trail of Bits that pairs AI-assisted security research from its most cyber-capable models with expert human review to find and patch vulnerabilities in critical open-source projects. Initial participants include cURL, Python, the Go project, pyca/cryptography, Sigstore, and aiohttp, with HackerOne and Calif supporting triage and coordinated disclosure.

title: Codex-maxxing for long-running work
blurb: This OpenAI whitepaper by Jason Liu lays out strategies for using Codex as a persistent workspace for projects that run past a single prompt. It covers breaking ambitious goals into verifiable steps, preserving context and continuity across workstreams, and deciding when to delegate execution to Codex versus when human oversight matters most.

title: Texas lassoes massive Microsoft datacenter - and 20 years of gas turbine emissions
blurb: Texas approved a large Microsoft datacenter that will be powered by on-site gas turbines permitted to emit at high levels for the next 20 years. The arrangement lets the facility sidestep grid constraints by generating its own power, but locks in two decades of fossil-fuel emissions to feed rising AI compute demand.

title: SpaceX inks compute deal with Reflection AI, an open source AI lab
blurb: Reflection AI signed a compute deal with SpaceX worth up to $6.3B, paying $150M a month from July 2026 through 2029 for access to Nvidia's GB300 chips at the Colossus 2 data center near Memphis. The open-weight lab, founded in 2024 by two former Google DeepMind researchers, framed the contract as one of the largest open AI infrastructure commitments to date.

title: Google Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer to join IPO-bound OpenAI
blurb: Noam Shazeer, co-lead of Google's Gemini and co-author of the original Transformer paper, is leaving Google to join OpenAI as the company moves toward an IPO. His departure removes a central architect of Gemini from Google's roster and adds a foundational researcher to OpenAI ahead of its public offering.

title: Server-Side Tools Are Now Available for DigitalOcean Inference Engine
blurb: DigitalOcean released Server-Side Tools in Public Preview for its Inference Engine, letting a model call web search, web fetch, Knowledge Bases, MCP servers, and supported Anthropic and OpenAI tools from inside a single inference request. The tools work with an existing Model Access Key, with no separate tool infrastructure or orchestration layer to operate, and web search and fetch are powered by Exa under usage-based pricing.

title: How Anthropic may have talked itself into an AI export ban
blurb: An FT analysis found that five in every 1,000 words from Anthropic in 2026 related to risk, regulation, or restrictions, eight times the rate for OpenAI and Sam Altman. Critics including Yann LeCun blame that messaging for Washington's decision last week to bar foreign nationals from using Anthropic's newest models, Mythos and Fable.

title: SpaceX Strikes $6 Billion Deal With AI Startup for Data-Center Space
blurb: SpaceX struck a $6B deal to rent data-center capacity to Reflection AI, the startup building open-source models. The agreement marks a notable expansion of the newly public company's infrastructure business and gives Reflection the compute it needs to train and develop its models at scale.

title: Nvidia unveils Vera Rubin platform targeting AI, HPC infrastructure
blurb: Nvidia launched the Vera Rubin platform at ISC High Performance 2026, a rack-scale supercomputer combining Vera CPUs, Rubin GPUs, NVLink-C2C interconnects, ConnectX-9 SuperNICs, and BlueField-4 DPUs for AI and scientific HPC workloads. A fully configured system supports up to 144 GPUs per rack and delivers over seven exaflops of AI performance alongside five petaflops of native FP64 compute, with 2.8 times the memory bandwidth of Blackwell.

title: NSF slashes research programs to support new tech initiative, insiders say
blurb: The National Science Foundation is cutting funding for existing research programs to redirect resources toward a new technology initiative, according to insiders. The reported shift would prioritize emerging tech areas at the expense of established grants, raising concerns among researchers about the stability of long-term funding across the broader science community.

title: Nvidia says its AI data center design runs hotter to use a lot less water
blurb: Nvidia claims its Rubin reference design for fully liquid-cooled data centers cuts water use from roughly 2.6 million gallons per megawatt per year to near zero by running servers as hot as 113°F. The design captures heat at the chip through liquid loops, though Nvidia's blog doesn't disclose build costs versus air cooling and leaves construction and power-generation concerns unaddressed.

title: Sources: Meta internally exposed data from its employee-tracking program meant to help train its AI models, including full prompts and private conversations (Wired)
blurb: Meta internally exposed data from an employee-tracking program built to train its AI models, including full prompts and private conversations, according to sources cited by Wired. The exposure raises fresh privacy concerns about how the company collects and handles worker data used in model development.

title: Groq raised $650M led by Disruptive and Infinitum after its Nvidia deal, aiming to hit 200 MW in capacity by the end of 2027, following a $750M raise in 2025 (Zsana Hoskins/Bloomberg)
blurb: Groq raised $650M led by Disruptive and Infinitum, following its recent Nvidia deal and a $750M round in 2025. The funding targets an expansion to 200 MW of inference capacity by the end of 2027, scaling the company's LPU-based hardware to compete on cost against Nvidia GPUs.

title: Alphabet Shares Drop After Second AI Star Departs for Rival
blurb: Alphabet shares fell Monday after a second high-profile AI leader left the company for a rival in recent weeks. The back-to-back departures fueled investor concern over Alphabet's ability to retain top research talent as competition for senior AI staff intensifies across frontier labs.

title: Alphabet has its worst day in over a year on AI concerns after high-profile exits
blurb: Alphabet shares closed down about 5% on Monday, its worst day in over a year, as two prominent researchers left for rivals and AI commoditization fears grew. Gemini co-lead Noam Shazeer departed for OpenAI and AlphaFold co-creator John Jumper left for Anthropic, raising questions about whether Google's $141B in AI spending since October is buying a durable advantage.

title: SpaceX signs computing power deal with open-source AI startup Reflection worth up to $6.3 billion
blurb: SpaceX signed a computing power deal worth up to $6.3 billion with open-source AI startup Reflection, giving the lab immediate access to Nvidia GB300 chips on its Colossus infrastructure. Reflection, last valued at $25 billion, will pay $150 million per month from July 2026 through 2029, joining Anthropic, Google, and Cursor as outside customers of SpaceX's data center build-out.

title: Researchers introduce Self-Harness, a framework that lets AI agents rewrite their own rules, boosting performance up to 60%
blurb: Researchers at the Shanghai Artificial Intelligence Laboratory introduced Self-Harness, a framework in which an LLM-based agent improves its own operating rules by examining its execution traces and applying empirical edits rather than manual debugging. The approach targets harness components like system prompts, verification rules, and failure-recovery procedures, and reported performance gains of up to 60% on model-specific weaknesses.

title: We Analyzed 137K Sites: 97% of llms.txt Files Never Get Read
blurb: An Ahrefs study of 137K domains found that 97% of published llms.txt files received zero traffic in May 2026, and 96% of the requests that did arrive came from bots rather than AI assistants. The findings align with Google's John Mueller, who called llms.txt a "temporary crutch" for AI coding tools parsing developer docs rather than something most sites need.

title: Inside Claude Managed Agents: Reverse Engineering the Security Boundaries of Anthropic's Hosted Agent Runtime
blurb: Pluto Security reverse-engineered Claude Managed Agents, Anthropic's hosted runtime where Claude runs autonomously in cloud containers with bash, file I/O, web access, and MCP connections. The analysis found gVisor isolation with three-layer egress control and a vault proxy that keeps secrets out of the sandbox, but also a readable egress JWT and an allowlist silently expanded with six Anthropic hosts beyond what users configure.

title: Midjourney, the AI image generator, is developing a full-body ultrasonic scanner
blurb: Midjourney, known for its text-to-image generator, announced Midjourney Medical and a first hardware product, the Scanner, a full-body ultrasonic machine built with Butterfly Network that aims to produce MRI-quality 3D body maps in under 60 seconds. The company plans to operate spa locations housing the scanners, and will spend the next 12 months running research trials and refining the algorithms and second-generation hardware.

title: Mistral AI to get Code and Apps features on Vibe
blurb: Mistral AI is building Code and Apps sections into the web version of Le Chat (Vibe), adding a browser coding surface that mirrors its CLI agents and an artifacts-style area for building, hosting, and sharing apps via connectors and multi-step workflows. Both features remain in development as CEO Arthur Mensch confirmed a large, sparse mixture-of-experts model shipping this summer as open weights, with partner early access in July.

title: Anthropic’s new privacy policy collects biometric data from flagged Claude users
blurb: Anthropic updated its privacy policy to require some flagged Claude users to upload government IDs and selfies that generate facial geometry templates, a data type that may qualify as biometric information under state laws. The requirement, effective July 8, applies to accounts flagged for potential policy violations and is processed by Persona, an identity firm backed by Founders Fund.

title: Solving an ARD problem in AI: Agentic Resource Discovery
blurb: Google, Microsoft, Cisco, Nvidia, Salesforce, and others proposed Agentic Resource Discovery (ARD), an open protocol that lets AI agents find and safely use the tools they need within a corporate domain. ARD works across two layers: organizations publish catalogs of available capabilities, and registries crawl those catalogs like a search engine. The specification is available now.

title: Doctors Thought It Was Asthma. A.I. Flagged a Serious Heart Problem.
blurb: An AI model that detects structural heart disease in standard ECGs, catching patterns that clinicians routinely miss, is being released for free to doctors. The program identified serious cardiac damage in patients whose symptoms had been attributed to asthma, pointing to wider screening for conditions that current diagnostic workflows overlook.

title: Nvidia tops Ethernet data center switch charts by revenue as sales soar
blurb: Nvidia became the top data center Ethernet switch vendor by revenue in Q1 2026, with sales up 192.7% year over year to $2.1B for a 21.5% segment share, per IDC's Quarterly Ethernet Switch Tracker. Demand for its Spectrum-X platform from hyperscalers building AI factories drove the gains, though Cisco still leads overall Ethernet switching with a 29.3% share.

title: Troubleshoot formula errors quickly with Gemini in Google Sheets
blurb: Gemini in Google Sheets can now diagnose and explain formula errors, rolling out to Workspace and consumer AI Pro/Ultra tiers starting June 22.

title: Don't rely on instructions, use Agent Hooks to enforce guardrails
blurb: This post argues that AGENTS.md or CLAUDE.md instructions are unreliable guardrails because agents sometimes ignore them, and recommends Agent Hooks for deterministic enforcement instead. It walks through two Claude Code examples, a PreToolUse hook that blocks raw <input> tags before the tool runs and a stop hook that prevents the agent from declaring done while a design-system ratchet test fails, parsing the JSON each hook receives on stdin with jq.

title: Sakana AI launches Fugu, a multi-agent orchestration system accessible through a single model API, claiming Fugu Ultra matches Fable and Mythos on benchmarks (Carl Franzen/VentureBeat)
blurb: Sakana AI launched Fugu, a multi-agent orchestration system that exposes coordinated agents through a single model API. The company claims its top tier, Fugu Ultra, matches Fable and Mythos on benchmarks while simplifying integration for developers who would otherwise wire together multiple agents themselves.

title: Local Qwen isn't a worse Opus, it's a different tool
blurb: The author argues that local Qwen models aren't a cheaper substitute for Opus but a distinct tool with a different cost-benefit profile, drawing on real numbers from running a bootstrapped software business. An RTX 6000 Pro card paid for itself within two or three months on his specific Go and infrastructure workloads, but Qwen still can't be trusted unsupervised. Its worst failure modes, infinite loops and hallucination, surface most once quantized down to fit a consumer GPU.

title: Google Cloud publishes a secure MCP-on-GKE guide
blurb: Google Cloud published a guide for deploying a secure remote MCP server on Google Kubernetes Engine using the Streamable HTTP transport, which lets one stateless server handle multiple client connections over a single HTTP endpoint. The walkthrough centers on GKE Autopilot for horizontal scaling and the Kubernetes Gateway API with SSL certificates to enforce encrypted, authorized-only traffic to a centrally shared server.

title: OpenAI prepares GPT-5.6 models for the upcoming release
blurb: OpenAI is preparing to release its GPT-5.6 family as soon as Tuesday, with standard, Mini, and Pro variants reportedly landing together and a context window pushed toward 1.5 million tokens, up from GPT-5.5's million. Developers testing early builds claim it edges out Anthropic's Mythos line on agentic coding, and OpenAI is said to be planning deeper price cuts on tokens to widen its existing lead.

title: Snowflake and the Agentic Resource Discovery Specification
blurb: Snowflake added support for the Agentic Resource Discovery specification, an open protocol with Microsoft and GoDaddy that standardizes how enterprises catalog and discover AI agents.

title: Alibaba's AI video model rises to No. 2 in global rankings, as OpenAI's Sora and ByteDance's Seedance fall away
blurb: Alibaba Cloud released HappyHorse 1.1, an API-first video generation model now live on Model Studio with a 40% launch discount, after the previous version reached No. 2 across all three Artificial Analysis Video Arena leaderboards. The upgrade lands as competitors retreat, with OpenAI discontinuing Sora over costs and ByteDance shelving Seedance 2.0's international rollout amid Hollywood copyright complaints.

title: Google DeepMind and A24 announce first-of-its-kind research partnership
blurb: Google DeepMind and A24 announced a research partnership pairing the lab with the film studio to develop new AI tools and workflows for filmmakers, alongside an undisclosed Google investment in A24. The collaboration spans multiple projects over time, embedding DeepMind's models directly in A24's creative process so its artists can shape the technology while giving researchers feedback from working filmmakers.

title: Give GitHub Copilot CLI real code intelligence with language servers
blurb: This post explains how to install and configure Language Server Protocol (LSP) servers for GitHub Copilot CLI, replacing the agent's brute-force grep and bytecode-extraction workarounds with structured code intelligence. An LSP Setup skill automates server installation across 14 languages, giving the agent precise go-to-definition, find-references, and type resolution instead of text-search heuristics that miss generics, overloads, and compiled code.

title: Four Ways We're Using Our MCP Server at Figma
blurb: Figma shared four internal workflows its teams run with the Figma MCP server, including refreshing living decks and shipping designs to production.

title: We built a persistent agent memory layer on Elasticsearch with 0.89 recall
blurb: Elastic's Search Labs detailed an agent memory layer built on Elasticsearch using three indices structured around cognitive-science memory categories, with hybrid recall via RRF and a cross-encoder reranker, plus supersession for contradictions, decay, and per-user document-level security. On a QA-style evaluation of 168 questions, the system averaged 0.89 recall at 10 with zero cross-tenant leaks.

title: Announcing Stack Overflow for Agents
blurb: Stack Overflow introduced Stack Overflow for Agents, an API-first knowledge exchange that lets AI coding agents share verified solutions instead of working in isolation. The beta targets what the company calls the Ephemeral Intelligence Gap, where agents repeatedly burn compute and tokens rediscovering the same bug fixes and architectural patterns that other agents have already solved.

title: iOS 27 beta 2 includes Write with Siri, the new version of Apple Intelligence writing tools
blurb: iOS 27 beta 2 replaces Apple Intelligence's Writing Tools with Write with Siri, a natural-language system for generating, proofreading, and rewriting text.

title: Atlassian's DESIGN.md is Here: What We Learned Testing Portable Design Context in Practice
blurb: Atlassian tested Google's open-source DESIGN.md format and found its portability trades off against the sophistication of dedicated design-context tooling.

title: Anthropic says Claude may want to see your ID
blurb: Anthropic updated its privacy policy to allow asking flagged users to verify their age or identity by uploading a government-issued ID, a selfie, and a face geometry template. The company says the requirement, effective July 8, applies only to a small subset of accounts flagged for potential fraud and gives them a path to appeal rather than face an outright ban.

title: Getty Images accused AI of wholesale theft. It’s now an official ChatGPT image partner.
blurb: Getty Images signed a licensing deal with OpenAI to bring its professionally licensed photos and visual assets into ChatGPT's search and discovery experiences. The agreement marks a reversal for Getty, which has been one of the loudest critics of training on copyrighted work and is currently suing AI firms, and gives OpenAI access to one of the largest collections of professional photography, journalism, and archival imagery.

title: Apertus – Open Foundation Model for Sovereign AI
blurb: The Swiss AI Initiative released Apertus, an open foundation model developed by EPFL, ETH Zurich, and CSCS, with fully documented training data, code, weights, and alignment methods built to meet EU AI Act requirements around opt-outs, PII removal, and memorization. The models are competitive with top open releases at 8B and 70B parameters and are multilingual from the start, trained on over 1,000 languages.

title: Introducing the MDN MCP server
blurb: Mozilla released the MDN MCP server, which brings MDN's web platform documentation and browser compatibility data directly into MCP-compatible coding agents and IDEs. The experimental server works with VS Code, Zed, Cursor, Claude Code, and Claude Desktop, giving agents access to current baseline support data rather than relying on potentially outdated training knowledge.

title: Building TREX: Code execution and artifact generation for AI code review
blurb: Greptile built TREX (Test, Run, Execute), an execution layer for AI code review that runs the code under review instead of only reading the diff. The system catches runtime bugs that static analysis misses, like logic errors requiring specific state, post-load UI regressions, and race conditions, and shares the main Greptile reviewer's context rather than running as a separate agent to avoid duplicated exploration and wasted compute.

title: Why cloud native belongs at the heart of agentic AI: Lessons from building a multi-agent security platform on Kubernetes
blurb: The author argues that cloud native infrastructure is the right foundation for production agentic AI, drawing on a multi-agent security platform being built at Orange Innovation on Kubernetes. Each agent runs as its own Deployment with isolated resource limits and identity, coordinated via the A2A protocol and LangGraph, with Falco and eBPF feeding syscall events through Kafka into an anomaly model that pre-filters before the LLM-driven agents act.

title: Tesla plans to sell modular AI data center hardware called ‘Megapod'
blurb: Tesla filed an intent-to-use trademark for "Megapod," a self-contained modular data center system bundling AI servers, networking, power distribution, and cooling sold as a single turnkey unit. The filing lands less than a year after Tesla killed Dojo, its in-house training computer, and pushes it into a rack-scale market dominated by Nvidia's GB200 NVL72 and the Dell and Supermicro systems built on it.

title: Microsoft’s Satya Nadella: We Can’t Let AI Giants Eat the Economy
blurb: Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella argued that AI companies must earn society's permission to operate rather than concentrate economic power among a few dominant players. He warned against a future where AI giants capture outsized shares of the economy, framing broad value creation and public trust as preconditions for the technology's long-term legitimacy.

title: Google tests literature review matrix tool for NotebookLM
blurb: Google is testing a "Lit Review" tool for NotebookLM that turns uploaded sources into a structured comparison matrix, currently in pre-release with no timeline.

title: Robinhood launches agentic trading via MCP server for all customers
blurb: Robinhood launched agentic trading through a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server, making it available to all customers. The integration lets AI agents connect directly to Robinhood accounts to execute trades and access portfolio data, extending agent-driven workflows into retail brokerage for the first time at this scale.

title: Only half of US datacenter capacity planned for 2026 is actually  under construction
blurb: A report found that only about half of the US datacenter capacity slated to come online in 2026 is currently under construction, suggesting a wide gap between announced buildout plans and physical reality. The shortfall raises questions about whether projected AI compute supply will materialize on schedule, even as demand forecasts and capital commitments continue to climb.

title: Why the Human Genome's Tangled Physicality May Confound AI
blurb: The author argues that the human genome resists the kind of understanding biologists once expected, since barely 2% of its 3 billion bases code for genes and the real difficulty lies in how those genes are regulated. Genomic foundation models like Evo 2, Genos, and DeepMind's AlphaGenome sidestep that complexity by learning correlations from known cases rather than mechanisms. The piece contends this will likely prove useful for prediction while leaving the deeper question of how the genome works inside a computational black box.

title: Anthropic's Mythos mess just keeps getting more complicated
blurb: The Trump administration issued an export control barring Anthropic's Mythos 5 and its Fable 5 derivative from use by any foreign national, including Anthropic's own employees, prompting the company to pull both models for all customers. The Register's reporting examines Amazon's possible role in justifying the move and notes that open-weight models will likely replicate the same capabilities regardless of the restriction.

title: When I reject AI code even if it works
blurb: The author argues that as AI accelerates implementation, the real bottleneck shifts to reviewing code the developer never reasoned through, producing cognitive overload that makes him reject working changes and start over. He rejects AI code when he can't explain the approach in his own words, when the diff exceeds the problem, or when it adds unproven abstractions, advocating mandatory human review alongside AI checks.

title: Preliminary Thoughts On The Midjourney Scanner
blurb: Midjourney announced a pivot from AI image generation to medical imaging with the Midjourney Scanner, a water tank ringed with ultrasound emitters that reconstructs 3D ultrasound tomography in twenty seconds without radiation. The author, a psychiatrist, argues the SF AI narrative has outrun the medical facts: ultrasound can't penetrate bone or air, ruling out the brain, lungs, and bowels that most CTs and MRIs target. The realistic niche is low-risk screening, whose value remains unproven.

title: Enable Real-Time AI for High-Speed Data Acquisition with DAQIRI
blurb: NVIDIA released DAQIRI (Data Acquisition for Integrated Real-time Instruments), a high-performance networking library in the Holoscan Platform that connects high-bandwidth streaming detectors directly to the NVIDIA software stack. It lets developers run filtering, inference, compression, and event selection in stream over a ConnectX NIC, replacing the traditional collect-store-analyze pipeline that breaks down at rates like LCLS-II's 1 MHz output.

title: Google forms research partnership with A24 Films that’s focused on AI filmmaking tools
blurb: Google is investing about $75 million in independent studio A24, giving the film company access to its DeepMind research lab to develop AI tools for the production process. A24 partner Scott Belsky said the work will focus on enhancing workflows like storyboarding rather than generating movies from scratch, marking Google's first investment in a film studio.

title: Do AI Assistants Actually Render Your JavaScript when Grounding? We Put It to the Test
blurb: A test of 12 AI assistants found that most read only raw HTML and miss JavaScript-rendered content when grounding on a pasted link.

title: SpaceX to buy Cursor AI coding agent operator Anysphere for $60B
blurb: SpaceX agreed to acquire Anysphere, the company behind the Cursor AI coding agent, for $60B. The deal would fold one of the most widely used AI coding tools into Elon Musk's space and satellite empire, signaling SpaceX's push to build out internal AI software capabilities and developer infrastructure.

title: Report: GKE Inference Gateway delivers up to 92% faster AI responses
blurb: Google's GKE Inference Gateway uses prefix caching and model-aware routing to direct LLM requests to accelerators already holding the relevant KV cache in memory, skipping recomputation of repeated prompt prefixes. An independent benchmark found it delivered 15.7% higher throughput, 92.8% shorter wait times, and 62.6% lower inter-token latency than the next leading managed Kubernetes service, with Snap reporting prefix cache hit rates of 75-80%.

title: Stripe and AWS enable AI agent payments for content owners and publishers
blurb: Stripe and AWS introduced infrastructure that lets AI agents make payments on behalf of users while compensating the content owners and publishers whose material the agents access. The integration combines Stripe's payment rails with AWS Bedrock and AgentCore, giving publishers a way to meter and monetize agent traffic rather than have their content scraped for free.

title: SpaceX Kicks Off High-Grade Bond Sale | Bloomberg Tech 6/22/2026
blurb: SpaceX launched its first investment-grade bond sale following its record IPO, its debut in the high-grade debt market.

title: Superconductor review: the cleanest way I've found to run AI agents in parallel
blurb: Superconductor is a native macOS, Rust-built workspace for running coding agents like Claude Code and Codex in parallel across projects, agent-agnostic.

title: NVIDIA announces 35 new AI HPC supercomputers across Europe
blurb: NVIDIA announced 35 new AI HPC supercomputers opening across Europe this year, built on its Blackwell and Hopper architectures and serving more than three million researchers. The systems span projects like Barcelona's MareNostrum 5 upgrade and Italy's IT4LIA AI factory, supporting research in climate science, healthcare, clean energy, and quantum computing.

title: 🎙️ How I AI: How to write AI agent loops in Claude Code and Codex + How Claude Mythos found a 15-year-old bug in Mozilla Firefox
blurb: A How I AI tutorial breaking down heartbeats, crons, hooks, and goal-based agent loops, with real automations built in Claude Code and Codex.

title: Evals: the strategic IP that will define the next era of AI
blurb: Garrett Lord argues that internal evaluations, not models, are becoming the strategic IP that will determine which companies win the next era of AI. Drawing on conversations with hundreds of execs frustrated by weak AI ROI, he contends that proprietary evals encoding a company's quality standards are what turn general-purpose models into reliable, differentiated production systems.

title: The PIK Fuse: How Private Equity Software Deals Like Medallia Actually Blow Up, and Exactly Who’s Next
blurb: Private equity loaded a dozen software leaders with debt at the 2021 peak, and the PIK toggle is what hides the damage until it's terminal. Payment-in-kind lets a borrower defer cash interest and roll it onto the principal, so the balance compounds while the business weakens and covenant tests still pass right up until the deferral window closes. Thoma Bravo just handed Medallia to its lenders, wiping out $5.1B in equity on a company earning $200M a year against $300M in debt service. It is the second giant wipeout in 18 months, and the fuse runs the same way every time.

title: Qualcomm Nears Deal for AI Chip Startup Modular
blurb: Qualcomm is in advanced talks to buy AI chip startup Modular at a roughly $4B valuation, pushing the mobile-chip giant deeper into the data center fight against Nvidia. Modular's pitch was always software, a unified layer meant to run AI workloads across any hardware, which is exactly what Qualcomm lacks as it tries to sell inference silicon. The deal signals that the next round of competition is less about raw chips and more about owning the software stack that decides where AI runs.

title: Alphabet shares close down 5% on Monday following the departure of Google DeepMind VP John Jumper, the company's second top AI executive to leave in a week (Bloomberg)
blurb: Alphabet shares closed down 5% on Monday after Google DeepMind VP John Jumper departed, the company's second top AI executive to leave in a week. Jumper shared the 2024 Nobel Prize in Chemistry for AlphaFold, making him one of DeepMind's most prominent researchers. Back-to-back senior exits feed the market's worry that Google is losing the talent race at the exact moment competitors are paying record packages to poach it.

title: Air Space Intelligence won an $875M, 12-year FAA contract to develop AI tools that map flight trajectories and identify areas of congestion to reduce delays (Allyson Versprille/Bloomberg)
blurb: Air Space Intelligence won an $875M, 12-year FAA contract to build AI that maps flight trajectories and flags congestion before it turns into delays. The deal is one of the largest federal bets yet on applied AI inside legacy infrastructure, and it signals that agencies are now willing to write multi-year, nine-figure checks for software that optimizes systems they already run rather than replacing them. For founders selling into government, the path is widening for startups that target a specific operational bottleneck instead of pitching a sweeping platform.

title: Sources: Qualcomm is in advanced talks to acquire Modular in a transaction valuing the AI chip startup at ~$4B, up from a $1.6B valuation in September 2025 (Bloomberg)
blurb: Qualcomm is in advanced talks to acquire AI chip startup Modular at roughly $4B, more than double its $1.6B valuation from September 2025. The deal signals how aggressively non-Nvidia players are paying up for the software and hardware layer that makes AI inference cheaper to run at scale. For founders, it is another sign that the compute stack is consolidating fast, and the independent tooling you build on today may belong to a chipmaker by next year.

title: SpaceX signs a computing deal worth up to $6.3B with Reflection AI for access to Nvidia GB300s at Colossus 2; Reflection will pay $150M per month through 2029 (Deirdre Bosa/CNBC)
blurb: Reflection AI committed up to $6.3B to SpaceX for compute, paying $150M per month through 2029 to run on Nvidia GB300s at Colossus 2. The deal locks one AI lab into a four-year, fixed-cost compute contract before its own revenue can plausibly cover it, a bet that model demand keeps compounding faster than the bill. The signal for founders is that frontier compute is now sold like infrastructure leases, with multi-year minimums and counterparties that also happen to be building rival hardware empires.

title: Trump says he no longer views Anthropic as a national security threat after G7 meeting with CEO
blurb: Trump told Axios he no longer sees Anthropic as a national security threat and may ease the June 12 Commerce directive forcing the company to seek US approval before foreign nationals can touch its Fable 5 and Mythos 5 models. The reversal came days after a G7 meeting with Dario Amodei, capping a three-month standoff that started when the Pentagon flagged Anthropic as a supply-chain risk over its refusal to strip military guardrails. The thaw lands right as Anthropic sits on a confidential IPO filing, where regulatory overhang is exactly the kind of risk that scares public-market buyers.

title: Oracle Cut 21,000 Jobs in 12 Months, Says AI Replaced Some Roles
blurb: Oracle cut 21,000 jobs over the past 12 months, a deeper reduction than previously reported, and named AI as the reason some of those roles no longer exist. The detail that matters is the explicit attribution: a legacy enterprise vendor is now stating on the record that automation, not just restructuring, is shrinking headcount. Expect more incumbents to frame layoffs the same way, both because it's increasingly true and because "AI did it" reads better to investors than admitting the core business is slowing.

title: Groq Raises $650 Million to Aid in Pivot After Nvidia Deal
blurb: Groq raised $650M to expand its data center capacity and reposition from a chip startup into a full AI compute provider, following its recent deal with Nvidia. The pivot reflects where the margins are moving: selling inference capacity as a service rather than competing head-on in silicon against the company that just became a partner. For founders building on hosted inference, it signals more competition among compute suppliers and likely downward pressure on per-token pricing.

title: Google DeepMind bets $75M on AI’s future in Hollywood with A24 deal
blurb: Google DeepMind invested $75M in indie studio A24 to co-develop AI filmmaking tools, with Demis Hassabis framing it as a first-of-its-kind partnership that gives DeepMind direct feedback from working artists. A24, the studio behind "Everything Everywhere All At Once" and "Backrooms," joins a pattern already set by Netflix buying Affleck's InterPositive and Amazon's MGM standing up an AI production unit. The frontier labs are no longer just selling models to Hollywood, they are buying their way into the creative process to source the proprietary taste and feedback data that generic models lack.

title: AI-Services Startup Baseten Nabs $13 Billion Valuation
blurb: Baseten raised $1.5B in a Series F at a $13B valuation, co-led by Altimeter, to sell software and compute capacity to companies running lower-cost AI models. The bet is that as open and cheaper models close the gap with frontier ones, the money moves to whoever makes them easy to deploy and serve in production. Inference infrastructure is becoming its own venture category, separate from the labs, because the teams adopting AI increasingly want the tooling layer rather than another model to pick from.

title: AI chipmaker Groq confirms $650M raise, re-staffs after Nvidia’s $20B not-acqui-hire deal
blurb: Six months after Nvidia licensed Groq's chip IP and hired away its CEO and top executives in a $20B not-acqui-hire, Groq confirmed a $650M round led by Disruptive and Infinitum. Rather than wind down, the company pivoted to its neocloud business, which now runs 13 data centers and serves over five million developers, and restocked its bench with a new COO, CTO, and CPO. The not-acqui-hire is becoming a recurring exit shape, where a rival buys the IP and the talent while investors cash out, and the open question is whether the husk left behind can rebuild into a real business.

title: Inference chip startup Groq raises $650M to grow its cloud platform
blurb: Groq raised $650M to expand its inference cloud, seven months after licensing its LPU chip technology to Nvidia in a $20B deal that also took its founding CEO. The bet is that the value sits in operating the inference, not owning the silicon: Groq's platform now runs trillions of tokens a week for 5 million developers across 13 data centers. The proceeds go toward scaling capacity to 200 megawatts by 2027, even as Nvidia ships the same LPU 3 hardware and rival clouds can theoretically stand up competing services.

title: How Anthropic may have talked itself into an AI export ban
blurb: Anthropic spent 2026 warning about AI risk far more than any rival, and the bet may be backfiring. FT analysis found 5 of every 1,000 words from the company and Dario Amodei related to risk, regulation, or restrictions, eight times OpenAI's 0.6. Critics now blame that messaging for Washington's decision last week to bar foreign nationals from using Anthropic's newest models, Mythos and Fable. The fight is an early test of how the US will govern frontier models, and a warning that loud safety positioning can invite the regulation it asks for.

title: SpaceX inks compute deal with Reflection AI, an open source AI lab
blurb: SpaceX signed Reflection AI, an open-weight lab, to a compute deal worth up to $6.3 billion, $150 million a month through 2029 for immediate access to Nvidia GB300 chips at its Colossus 2 site near Memphis. It's the third major lab to rent SpaceX's idle capacity after Anthropic at $1.25 billion a month and Google at $920 million, a sign that the company's chip stockpile is now a bigger asset than its own faltering AI ambitions. Reflection is framing the deal as proof that open models are winning runway as nations and enterprises pull back from depending on closed labs.

title: The minimum viable unit of saleable software
blurb: LLMs flipped the buy-vs-build calculus, so the cheap internal package that replaces your $400/mo SaaS is now a real threat to anyone selling software. But cheap isn't zero, and the author's point is that LLM-built systems still demand dozens of feedback loops to get right plus an open-ended maintenance bill for every new feature and bug. The opening for saleable software is the work LLMs make easier but not free, where the ongoing cost of owning the thing yourself still outweighs the price of buying it.

title: Nvidia backs AI networking switch silicon startup Upscale in $190M raise
blurb: Upscale AI raised $190M to build networking switch silicon aimed only at AI workloads, bringing total funding to $500M and its valuation to $2B barely a year after launching. The round was led by Premji Invest, but the notable name is Nvidia, which joined the extension even as it sells its own AI networking gear. A frontier supplier funding a startup that competes in part of its own stack signals how acute the cluster-scale networking bottleneck has become, and how much room there still is below the incumbents.

title: Sources: Vimeo owner Bending Spoons seeks to raise ~$1.62B in a US IPO, selling 58M shares at $26 to $28 apiece, at a valuation of $19B at the top of the range (Echo Wang/Reuters)
blurb: Bending Spoons, the Italian app conglomerate behind Vimeo, Evernote, and WeTransfer, is seeking to raise about $1.62B in a US IPO, selling 58M shares at $26 to $28 for a valuation near $19B at the top of the range. The company built that scale by buying tired software businesses, cutting costs hard, and raising prices rather than chasing new product bets. Its IPO is a public test of whether the acquire-and-optimize playbook holds up as a standalone growth story, not just a private roll-up strategy.

title: SpaceX Strikes $6 Billion Deal With AI Startup for Data-Center Space
blurb: SpaceX signed a $6B deal to rent data-center capacity to Reflection AI, which will use it to train open-source models. The move puts a rocket company in the compute-landlord business and gives a young lab access to scale without building its own infrastructure. It is another sign that the AI buildout is being financed through long-term capacity contracts, where whoever controls power and data-center space increasingly sets the terms for the labs renting it.

title: Sources: marketing tech startup AppsFlyer raised a $1B Series E at a $2.7B post-money valuation; Moloco, Google, Meta, and Unity acquire minority stakes (Kerry Flynn/Axios)
blurb: AppsFlyer raised a $1B Series E at a $2.7B post-money valuation, with Moloco, Google, Meta, and Unity each taking minority stakes. The investor list is the real signal: the same ad platforms AppsFlyer measures are now on its cap table, betting on neutral attribution as signal loss from privacy changes makes independent measurement harder to fake. For founders in martech, it shows where the money is going as the post-cookie, post-IDFA scramble pushes measurement back toward third parties the big platforms can't fully see.

title: SpaceX Readies Its $20 Billion Bond Debut to Fund AI Ambition
blurb: SpaceX is selling investment-grade dollar bonds for the first time, expected to kick off a borrowing spree on top of its record $75B IPO. The capital is earmarked for AI ambitions, putting one of the world's most valuable private companies into the same compute-funding race as the frontier labs. When a launch business with reliable cash flow starts leveraging up to chase AI, it signals that the appetite for capital has outgrown what equity rounds alone can supply.

title: Revolut and Synthesia early backer Seedcamp raises $320M, invests in US
blurb: Seedcamp, the early backer of Revolut and Synthesia, raised $320M and is opening a dedicated New York office to build a "Transatlantic Bridge" for the European startups it funds. The capital splits into a $220M seventh first-check fund and a $100M growth fund aimed at Series B and beyond, with the US presence meant to plug founders into American capital, hires, and customers from day one. The bet is that Europe's best companies no longer wait for permission to go global, so their lead investor is meeting them on the ground in the US.

title: Groq raised $650M led by Disruptive and Infinitum after its Nvidia deal, aiming to hit 200 MW in capacity by the end of 2027, following a $750M raise in 2025 (Zsana Hoskins/Bloomberg)
blurb: Groq raised $650M led by Disruptive and Infinitum, just months after its Nvidia deal, and now plans to reach 200 MW of inference capacity by the end of 2027. The round follows a $750M raise in 2025, putting roughly $1.4B behind its bid to undercut Nvidia on inference economics. The capital is going into capacity, not just chips, which is the constraint that actually decides whether a challenger can win serving workloads at scale.

title: SpaceX signs computing power deal with open-source AI startup Reflection worth up to $6.3 billion
blurb: SpaceX signed a computing deal with open-source startup Reflection worth up to $6.3 billion, giving the $25 billion lab immediate access to Nvidia GB300s for $150 million a month through 2029. The deal turns Colossus, built to train Musk's Grok, into a compute landlord that already counts Anthropic, Google, and Cursor as customers. The timing matters: open-source momentum has spiked since Anthropic cut off Fable and Mythos, and Reflection is betting that governments and enterprises wary of closed-model lock-in will pay for models they can inspect, customize, and run themselves.

title: WhatsApp gets new chief as Meta taps India’s CRED founder Kunal Shah and invests $900M in startup
blurb: Meta named CRED founder Kunal Shah to run WhatsApp, replacing Will Cathcart after nearly seven years, and led a $900M round in CRED at the same time. The pairing is a bet on India, WhatsApp's largest market with more than 500 million of its 3 billion users, and the one place WhatsApp Pay gained traction before stalling out behind PhonePe and Google Pay. Meta is handing the world's biggest messaging app to an operator who built consumer fintech in that market, a signal that the next phase of growth is payments and commerce, not messaging.

title: SoftBank-Backed Robot Maker Gears Up for Hong Kong IPO, Sources Say
blurb: Coowa, the SoftBank-backed maker of robots, is preparing a Hong Kong IPO after raising more than $600 million in a round that valued it above $3 billion. The listing pulls a humanoid and service-robotics player into public markets while most of the field is still private and burning cash on hardware. For founders, it sets an early valuation benchmark for the category and signals that public-market appetite for embodied AI is real enough to test, not just venture-stage enthusiasm.

title: Revolut, Wise and UiPath-backer Seedcamp raises $320m
blurb: Seedcamp, the European early-stage firm behind Revolut, Wise, and UiPath, raised $320m across two new funds to keep writing first checks into the region's startups. The firm is leaning into the sovereignty wave sweeping European tech, but drawing a hard line: it won't back companies whose only differentiator is being local. The signal for founders is that capital is flowing again to European pre-seed, but the bar is the same as anywhere, and "we're sovereign" is a feature, not a business.

title: Building a ‘digital twin’ 10,000 feet underground: PNNL, Nvidia and Fervo team up on geothermal AI
blurb: Geothermal just got a serious compute backer: PNNL, Nvidia, and Fervo are building a publicly available digital twin that models reservoirs 10,000 feet down to optimize how operators drill, monitor, and inject water. Today's models are too slow to guide real-time decisions, which leaves heat in the ground and capacity stranded. The bet matters because data centers are starving for clean baseload power, and Fervo, fresh off $2.17B raised, is bringing a 500-megawatt Utah plant online this year while Nvidia supplies the infrastructure and Fervo the proprietary field data.

title: Tesla plans to sell modular AI data center hardware called ‘Megapod'
blurb: Tesla filed an intent-to-use trademark for "Megapod," a self-contained modular AI data center system bundling servers, networking, power distribution, and cooling into a single turnkey unit. The move comes less than a year after Tesla killed Dojo, its only in-house AI training computer, and pushes it into a market Nvidia already dominates with the GB200 NVL72 and DGX SuperPOD, the reference designs Dell and Supermicro both build on. Tesla would be selling the rack-and-room around chips it doesn't make, against the company whose silicon powers essentially all of it.

title: Kids’ Online Safety Bill Deal Struck by House GOP, Democrats
blurb: House Republicans and Democrats reached a bipartisan deal on a package strengthening online safety and privacy rules for minors, the clearest sign yet that federal kids-safety legislation is finally moving. The agreement clears the path for new obligations around how platforms handle data, design, and content for users under 18. Any startup with a consumer product that teens can reach should assume age verification, default privacy settings, and design-feature liability are about to become table stakes rather than optional, and the compliance work is cheaper to start now than after the rules land.

title: AppsFlyer Reportedly Lands $1B At $2.7B Valuation To Help Companies Track Digital Ads
blurb: AppsFlyer reportedly raised more than $1B at a $2.7B valuation, its first round since 2020 and a step the company says is on its path to the public markets. The telling detail is who wrote the checks: Moloco, Google, Meta, and Unity each took a minority stake, meaning the ad platforms being measured are now funding their own independent referee. As AI takes over how ads get bought and optimized, the attribution signals feeding those systems become infrastructure worth owning, which is why the measurement layer is suddenly drawing nine-figure rounds.

title: Bending Spoons prices IPO at up to $1.62 billion, targeting $19 billion Nasdaq valuation
blurb: Bending Spoons is pricing its Nasdaq IPO at $26 to $28 a share, seeking up to $1.62B at a $19B valuation for an early-July debut under the ticker BSP. The Italian firm runs a private-equity playbook on software, buying underperforming digital products like Vimeo, WeTransfer, AOL, Evernote, and Eventbrite, cutting costs, and running them at scale. The $19B mark nearly doubles the $11B it carried in late 2025, and the listing is shaping up as Europe's first big test of whether the US mega-IPO wave pulls the continent's software companies back to public markets.

title: Run isolated sandboxes with full lifecycle control: AWS Lambda introduces MicroVMs
blurb: AWS Lambda introduced MicroVMs, a serverless primitive that runs untrusted user- or AI-generated code in isolated, stateful environments powered by Firecracker, the same virtualization behind Lambda functions. Each MicroVM gives a session VM-level isolation with near-instant launch and resume, retains memory and disk state for the session, and pauses to low idle cost when the user steps away.

title: How we found a bug in the hyper HTTP library
blurb: Cloudflare engineers spent six weeks tracing intermittent truncation in their Images binding, where larger transformation requests returned a 200 status but delivered only a fraction of the expected image data. The root cause was a race condition in the hyper Rust HTTP library affecting how processed image data streamed back to the client, and the fix came down to four lines of code.

title: Researchers Detail DifyTap Flaws in Dify That Could Expose AI Chats Across Tenants
blurb: Zafran Security disclosed four vulnerabilities, collectively named DifyTap, in Dify, the open-source agentic workflow platform with over 146,000 GitHub stars. Two were critical and two required no authentication, with cross-tenant flaws that let attackers read other customers' private AI chats and uploaded documents on the multi-tenant cloud service using only a file UUID.

title: ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Attackers compromised ShapedPlugin's build and distribution pipeline, injecting backdoor code into Pro versions of Product Slider Pro for WooCommerce, Real Testimonials Pro, and Smart Post Show Pro delivered through the vendor's official licensed update channels. The malware fetches a remote payload on every admin page load, captures plaintext credentials and 2FA codes, exfiltrates wp-config.php and recent WooCommerce order data, and carries a CVSS 10.0 rating, while free WordPress.org builds are unaffected.

title: The text in Claude Code’s “Extended Thinking” output
blurb: The author argues that Claude Code's local "Extended Thinking" logs are not an authentic record of the model's reasoning, since Anthropic encrypts the actual thinking into a signature it holds the key to and returns only a lossy summary over the API. The practical takeaway for engineers is to stop treating those local files as an audit trail, because reconstructing the real logic that drove an agent's actions requires an enterprise agreement and is impossible from your machine alone.

title: Show HN: Oak – Git alternative designed for agents
blurb: Oak is an open-source version control system built for AI coding agents, structured as a Rust Cargo workspace with a reusable VCS library and a CLI that agents drive. It uses branch-per-session as the unit of work, branch descriptions instead of per-commit messages, and content-addressed lazy mounts that hydrate on demand, making it faster than git for agent workloads.

title: Steam Machine launches today
blurb: Valve's Steam Machine ships today, a compact living-room PC running SteamOS on Linux with Proton handling Windows game compatibility. The launch extends Valve's push to make Linux a viable gaming target, building on the Steam Deck's Proton layer that has steadily closed the compatibility gap with native Windows titles.

title: Making secret scanning more trustworthy: Reducing false positives at scale
blurb: GitHub partnered with Microsoft Security & AI's Agents Offense team to add LLM-based contextual verification to its secret scanning, borrowing the in-context reasoning approach from Agentic Secret Finder to judge whether a detected value is a real exposure rather than just pattern-matching it. The goal is to bring AI-detected generic secrets up to the same precision as provider-pattern detections, cutting low-value alerts that erode developer trust without sacrificing coverage.

title: Interactions API: our primary interface for Gemini models and agents
blurb: Google moved its Interactions API to general availability and made it the primary interface for calling Gemini models and running agents, replacing the December 2025 beta. The GA release adds Managed Agents that provision a remote Linux sandbox per call, background execution via background=True, mixed built-in and custom tools in one request, and Flex/Priority tiers that cut cost by up to 50%.

title: Prompt Injection as Role Confusion
blurb: The author argues that prompt injection is fundamentally a role-confusion problem: because an LLM receives system prompts, user messages, tool outputs, and its own reasoning as one undifferentiated token stream, it cannot reliably tell its own thoughts from injected text. Framing roles as the core abstraction lets the post derive new attacks, explain existing mech-interp results, and predict when an injection will succeed.

title: Adopting AV1 for Real-Time Communication (RTC) at Scale
blurb: Meta rolled out the AV1 video codec across the majority of mobile devices in its Messenger and WhatsApp real-time calling apps, after introducing it on high-end hardware in 2023. The post details the rate control and error resilience work behind the deployment, citing at least a 20% bitrate reduction over H.264/AVC on low- and mid-range devices and noticeably sharper video at the sub-100 kbps bitrates common in emerging markets.

title: GitHub Actions hardens checkout security to block ‘pwn request’ attacks
blurb: GitHub released actions/checkout v7, which automatically blocks and fails workflows that try to fetch unreviewed fork PR code under `pull_request_target` or `workflow_run` triggers, closing the "pwn request" hole that exposed repo secrets to attacker-controlled code. Starting July 16 the defaults backport to all supported major versions, so workflows pinned to a floating tag like `actions/checkout@v4` pick up the change automatically while SHA-pinned ones need a manual upgrade.

title: Lighthouse agentic browsing scoring
blurb: Chrome's Lighthouse added an Agentic Browsing category that runs deterministic audits on how well a site supports machine interaction, checking WebMCP tool registration, accessibility-tree integrity, and layout stability. Rather than a 0-100 score, it reports a fractional pass ratio plus per-audit pass/fail status, and the CDP-driven checks are reproducible enough to wire into CI/CD.

title: Cloudflare teams up with Chrome, Firefox, and Edge on a privacy-first anti-bot protocol
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, Microsoft, and Shopify are building PACT, a protocol that issues anonymous tokens to prove a visitor is a human or an authorized bot, replacing CAPTCHAs and forced logins without tracking. The tokens are designed so they cannot reconstruct browsing history, and the group plans to submit them for formal standardization as automated systems now make up roughly 58% of HTTP requests worldwide.

title: ClickHouse Ingestion at Scale: An Open-Source Zepto Engineering Story
blurb: Zepto rewrote core parts of the open-source ClickHouse Kafka Connect connector to add buffering and batching logic, boosting ingestion throughput by 45% while eliminating GC pauses and cutting ClickHouse insert pressure. The post traces how Confluent Cloud's managed black box blocked low-level broker and connector tuning at a sustained 10 MB/s, and details the two pull requests Zepto merged back upstream to fix the hidden performance killers.

title: Announcing DuckDB 1.5.4
blurb: DuckDB 1.5.4 (Variegata) is the fifth patch release in the 1.5 line, shipping bugfixes, security patches, and performance work alongside a parallel v1.4.5 LTS (Andium) release. Fixes include correctness bugs in VARIANT casts and MERGE INTO binding, several crash and memory-leak repairs in Arrow GeoArrow serialization and gzip writes, and allocator and Parquet stats-pruning improvements.

title: The minimum viable unit of saleable software
blurb: The author argues that LLMs have shifted the buy-vs-build calculus but not eliminated the case for selling software, since LLM-built systems still demand dozens of feedback loops to reach quality and carry ongoing maintenance cost. His bet on turning the River side project into a business rests on cheap not meaning free, leaving room for polished, well-maintained packages that internal LLM clones rarely match.

title: Codex-maxxing for long-running work
blurb: In this OpenAI whitepaper, Jason Liu lays out strategies for running Codex as a persistent workspace that preserves context and sustains progress across multi-prompt projects. The guide covers breaking goals into verifiable steps, maintaining continuity across parallel workstreams, and deciding when to delegate execution to Codex versus when to keep a human in the loop.

title: CCCL Runtime: A Modern C++ Runtime for CUDA
blurb: NVIDIA released CCCL runtime, a set of header-only C++ APIs (`<cuda/stream>`, `<cuda/buffer>`, `<cuda/launch>`) that reimplements core CUDA functionality like stream management, memory allocation, and kernel launches on top of the driver API. It targets the same role as the classic CUDA runtime but with modern C++ idioms for safer resource management and explicit dependencies, plus compatibility helpers for incremental adoption without rewriting surrounding CUDA runtime code.

title: Unsloth GLM-5.2 – How to Run Locally
blurb: Unsloth released Dynamic GGUF quants of Z.ai's GLM-5.2, a 744B-parameter MoE with 40B active parameters and a 1M context window, letting the open model run on local hardware down to a 239GB 2-bit build that fits a 256GB unified-memory Mac. The 2-bit quant holds ~82% accuracy at 84% smaller than the full 1.5TB model, with 4-bit and 5-bit dynamic quants measuring near-lossless on KL divergence.

title: AWS Continuum offers devs help with securing code
blurb: AWS released Continuum, a service that continuously discovers, investigates, and remediates vulnerabilities across first-party and third-party code, generating remediation recommendations and proposing fixes that route through existing review workflows. It adds new capabilities like STRIDE-format threat modeling on top of penetration testing and code scanning borrowed from Security Agent, plus an opt-in "enforce mode" that autonomously fixes code lapses once it has learned a team's guardrails.

title: Don't rely on instructions, use Agent Hooks to enforce guardrails
blurb: The author argues that AGENTS.md and CLAUDE.md instructions are unreliable guardrails because agents ignore them, and shows how Agent Hooks enforce rules deterministically instead. The post walks through two Claude Code hooks: a PreToolUse hook that blocks raw `<input>` tags by exiting with code 2 to feed stderr back as agent feedback, and a Stop hook that prevents the agent from claiming it's done while a design-system ratchet test fails.

title: window.showDirectoryPicker opens up a whole new world
blurb: Chrome's window.showDirectoryPicker() lets a website request read/write access to a directory on the user's filesystem, enabling local-first apps that operate on the user's own files instead of cloud storage. The author used it with Claude to build browser-based photo management and a node-based compositing tool inspired by Apple's Shake, all reading and writing source files directly on disk.

title: The Flat Curve Society
blurb: The author argues that as frontier models cross into dangerous capability, access to them will be restricted like nuclear weapons, with governments clamping down on the compute supply chain rather than the models themselves. Most companies and engineers will be left with capped, mediocre models or buy intelligence vending-machine style, sending specs to vendors who run the work on their own servers.

title: Metric Semantic Layer: How Lyft Governs and Scales Key Data Definitions
blurb: Lyft built an internal Metric Semantic Layer, a centralized Python package that stores every metric's plain-English description and definitive SQL in YAML configs and Jinja templates, ending the problem of teams using divergent definitions for the same metric. Updating a definition once propagates the change to every downstream application, with governance baked in through clear ownership, defined scope, and accountability that survives org changes and attrition.

title: Why Drawing Tablet Brands Won't Collaborate on Linux Floss Drivers
blurb: The author, who reviews drawing tablets on Linux, documents his failed push to get brands like Huion, XpPen, Gaomon, and Ugee to share hardware specs with the kernel's hid/input maintainers for open-source drivers. After reaching a technical contact who manages drivers across all four brands, he traces the wall to a business reality: the brands treat their proprietary driver structure as a competitive asset and see no upside in upstream FLOSS collaboration.

title: A 3D voxel game engine written in APL
blurb: A developer built an experimental 3D voxel game engine in APL, using the notation to drive a Vulkan, DirectX12, and Metal rendering backend.

title: I am dreading our LLM-written incident report future
