blurb: The author argues that using LLMs to write incident reports, not just gather their inputs, removes the thinking-while-writing step that forces engineers to confront whether their explanation actually fits the evidence. The result is a plausible, unchecked narrative that may invent system couplings or miss real interactions, which the author considers more dangerous than LLM coding because there is no test to catch the fabrication.

title: Optimizing Models to Be Fast at Codegen
blurb: Morph trains workload-specific speculative drafters on coding output, lifting acceptance from 1.93x to 3.07x and serving open models like Qwen and DeepSeek faster.

title: Running Async Python Inside Celery Is Harder Than You Think.
blurb: This post walks through why running async Python inside Celery is harder than wrapping tasks in asyncio.run(), which throws RuntimeError when a loop is already running and breaks subtly under nest_asyncio's re-entrant scheduling. It also covers the prefork trap, where forked workers inherit a parent event loop and hang in silence rather than erroring, plus the maintenance cost of keeping parallel sync and async code paths.

title: Superconductor review: the cleanest way I've found to run AI agents in parallel
blurb: Superconductor is a native macOS Rust app for running coding agents in parallel across projects, agent-agnostic with Claude Code, Codex, and others.

title: AWS enters the context layer race with a graph that learns from agents, not manual curation
blurb: AWS announced a context intelligence stack for AI agents, led by AWS Context, a knowledge graph service that automatically maps relationships across existing data and refines itself based on which sources agents actually use. It publishes all metadata as Apache Iceberg to S3 Tables queryable via Athena, Redshift, or Spark, and ships alongside GA of S3 Annotations and a preview of skill assets in Glue Data Catalog.

title: The analytics engineer in 2026: system designer, governance owner, AI context provider
blurb: dbt argues the analytics engineer's job has shifted from writing models to designing the system around them, as AI now handles scaffolding, first-draft docs, and boilerplate tests for the 72% of analytics engineers who code with AI assistance. The remaining work is the judgment AI can't do: defining metric grain, drawing domain boundaries, and structuring the semantic layer so downstream AI queries stay consistent. Governance outputs like contracts, tests, and column-level lineage become the role's measured deliverable.

title: Boost BigQuery with Python: Managed Python UDFs now generally available
blurb: Google made BigQuery's Managed Python UDFs generally available, letting teams run custom Python with libraries like NumPy, pandas, and scikit-learn directly inside SQL queries or BigFrames on serverless, auto-scaling compute. BigQuery handles container builds, security patching, and execution, so procedural logic, scientific computation, and external API enrichment that used to need separate infrastructure now run next to the data without managing images or compute services.

title: The new database world according to Google: Inexact queries and AI in everything
blurb: Google's database leadership laid out a roadmap built on AI-embedded tooling and "inexact" queries, with a product exec predicting that humans won't directly use data platforms within three to five years. The bet is that agents, not analysts, become the primary query interface, pushing vendors toward natural-language and approximate-result systems rather than the exact, schema-bound SQL that data teams build on today.

title: The semantic debt crisis no one is talking about
blurb: The author argues that organizations carry "semantic debt" alongside technical debt: every metric built without a canonical definition, every filter hardcoded into a dashboard instead of the data layer, is a withdrawal that compounds until three teams present three defensible-but-conflicting numbers for the same metric. The fix is encoding business meaning into the data itself through a semantic layer, which the piece frames as the foundation that makes AI reliable in production rather than a modeling nicety.

title: Clap Studio creates "the most music-respectful room" in Spook nightclub
blurb: Clap Studio renovated the El Cubo room at Valencia's Spook nightclub by borrowing acoustic principles from "dead rooms," using sound-absorbing surfaces and minimal reflective materials to prioritize music clarity over visual spectacle. The project, a follow-up to the studio's work on sister club Oven, treats acoustics as the primary design constraint rather than an afterthought layered onto a finished interior.

title: iOS 27 beta 2 includes Write with Siri, the new version of Apple Intelligence writing tools
blurb: Apple's iOS 27 beta 2 replaces Writing Tools with Write with Siri, swapping the old panel of preset buttons like 'Friendly' and 'Professional' for a natural-language input that expands from the Dynamic Island and pulls in personal context. The new system handles generation, proofreading, and rewrites with a before/after review panel, and works by voice or keyboard across iOS, iPadOS 27, and macOS Golden Gate ahead of a fall release.

title: Here’s what’s new with iOS 27 beta 2
blurb: Apple released iOS 27 beta 2 to developers, the first follow-up since the WWDC debut, with refinements to Liquid Glass, a faster Siri AI, and a "Write with Siri" keyboard suggestion that replaces Writing Tools. The build feels further along than typical second betas, signaling that Apple is treating stability as a headline feature ahead of the July public beta and September release.

title: Why Drawing Tablet Brands Won't Collaborate on Linux Floss Drivers
blurb: Artist David Revoy explains why Huion, XPPen, Gaomon, and Ugee won't help build open-source Linux drivers: the four brands share a single technical team in Shenzhen, and contributing specs to the kernel's HID/input maintainers offers them no commercial upside while exposing how interchangeable their hardware is. The piece is a clear-eyed look at why community driver work still falls to volunteers dumping specs by hand, the way Wacom's decades of upstream support remains the lonely exception.

title: Apple’s New CEO Must Restore Company’s Design Edge: Power On
blurb: In his Power On newsletter, Bloomberg's Mark Gurman argues that new Apple CEO John Ternus should make rebuilding the design team and refocusing on product look and feel his top priority. Gurman frames the past few years of hardware and software design as a drift from the craft that defined Apple under Jony Ive, positioning a design reset as the clearest test of Ternus's early tenure.

title: A New Era of Midjourney
blurb: Midjourney announced a pivot beyond image generation into medical hardware, building a full-body Ultrasonic CT scanner that uses a ring of roughly 500,000 grain-sized sensors to produce MRI-quality 3D maps in a 60-second scan. The company plans to open a spa-styled scanning room in San Francisco in 2027 and scale to a fleet of 50,000 scanners handling a billion scans a month by 2031.

title: FFmpeg fixes PixelSmash flaw in widely used video decoder
blurb: FFmpeg patched PixelSmash (CVE-2026-8461, CVSS 8.8), a heap out-of-bounds write in the MagicYUV decoder triggered by a malicious AVI, MKV, or MOV file processed through libavcodec. JFrog achieved full RCE against a Jellyfin 10.11.9 server via its automatic library scan; Kodi, OBS, PhotoPrism, Nextcloud, and desktop thumbnail generators are also exposed, with RCE viable when ASLR is disabled or chained around.

title: Nearly half of LG smart TV apps contain residential proxy SDKs
blurb: Researchers at Spur scanned 6,038 LG and Samsung smart TV apps and found 2,058 of them, nearly half on LG, bundling residential proxy SDKs from vendors like Bright Data that route strangers' internet traffic through the TV's home connection. The SDKs ask for consent once during setup and keep proxying after the app is closed, often with the proxy company itself acting as publisher, making these devices long-lived, unaudited exit nodes on home networks.

title: A new unpatchable flaw in Apple chips opens the door to an iPhone jailbreak
blurb: Paradigm Shift, a Barcelona offensive-security firm, published "usbliter8," a Boot ROM flaw with a proof-of-concept affecting Apple's A12 and A13 chips in the iPhone XS, XR, and 11. Because the bug sits in immutable, burned-in code it cannot be patched, but exploitation requires physical access and must be chained with further bugs to achieve a full jailbreak or forensic unlock.

title: Jaredfromsubway.eth, Ethereum's Most Active Sandwich Bot, Drained for $7.5M Over the Weekend
blurb: An attacker drained over $7.5 million in WETH, USDC, and USDT from jaredfromsubway.eth, the Ethereum address responsible for roughly 70% of all sandwich attacks on the network, using what Blockaid called a counter-MEV honeypot rather than phishing or a private-key compromise. It is the largest single-event loss publicly tied to an MEV operator, and some proceeds have already moved through Tornado Cash.

title: Microsoft fixes AutoGen Studio flaw that enabled code execution
blurb: Microsoft patched AutoJack, a three-flaw chain in AutoGen Studio that let a malicious webpage drive a developer's browsing agent into running arbitrary PowerShell, Bash, or executables on the host via the unauthenticated localhost MCP WebSocket. Exposure was limited to those building from the main GitHub branch between the MCP plugin commit and the hardening fix; PyPI releases, including autogenstudio 0.4.2.2, were never affected.

title: OpenAI unveils an updated GPT-5.5-Cyber model, launches the Patch the Planet initiative in partnership with Trail of Bits to fix open source bugs, and more (Lily Hay Newman/Wired)
blurb: OpenAI released an updated GPT-5.5-Cyber model and launched Patch the Planet, a program with Trail of Bits to find and fix bugs in widely used open source projects. The model targets vulnerability discovery and remediation workflows, and the initiative aims to feed AI-generated patches back upstream, raising the familiar questions about triage load and patch quality for maintainers receiving them.

title: Researchers Detail DifyTap Flaws in Dify That Could Expose AI Chats Across Tenants
blurb: Zafran Security disclosed four vulnerabilities, collectively named DifyTap, in Dify, the open-source agentic workflow platform, two of them critical (CVE-2026-41948 at CVSS 9.4, CVE-2026-41947 at 9.1) and two requiring no authentication, that allow cross-tenant reads of other customers' AI chats and uploaded files on Dify's multi-tenant cloud. Operators on affected versions should patch immediately and treat prior cross-tenant conversation and document exposure as possible, since the flaws created a covert exfiltration channel.

title: ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Threat actors compromised ShapedPlugin's build and EDD distribution pipeline, backdooring Pro releases of Product Slider Pro for WooCommerce (CVE-2026-49777, CVSS 10.0), Real Testimonials Pro, and Smart Post Show Pro (incident-wide CVE-2026-10735, CVSS 9.8). The loader fetches a self-erasing payload that captures plaintext credentials and 2FA codes, exfiltrates wp-config.php and admin accounts, and drops a web shell; only Pro builds from account.shapedplugin.com are affected.

title: Daybreak: Tools for securing every organization in the world
blurb: OpenAI expanded its Daybreak program with new defensive tooling, including an updated Codex Security plugin that discovers and patches vulnerabilities, and the full release of GPT-5.5-Cyber, which scores 85.6% on CyberGym versus 81.8% for GPT-5.5, available through a limited release to trusted defenders. It also launched Patch the Planet with Trail of Bits and HackerOne, with cURL, Go, Python, and 30-plus open-source projects committed to AI-assisted patching.

title: OpenAI launches new security tools and updates GPT-5.5-Cyber
blurb: OpenAI launched a suite of defender tools, expanding Daybreak from bug discovery into patch automation via an updated Codex Security plugin and a limited release of GPT-5.5-Cyber, gated to verified defenders. Codex Security scans full codebases, commits, or diffs and exports findings as SARIF and CodeQL; GPT-5.5-Cyber scored 85.6% on CyberGym and 39.5% on ExploitGym, beating GPT-5.5 on both.

title: Anthropic’s powerful Mythos AI reportedly breached ‘almost all’ NSA classified systems within a few hours during red-team test — report sheds more light on the U.S. government's sudden ban on the flagship models
blurb: Anthropic's Mythos 5 model breached "almost all" NSA classified systems within hours during an authorized June 11 red-team exercise, according to NSA chief Gen. Joshua Rudd as relayed by Sen. Mark Warner and reported by The Economist. The result preceded a June 12 federal directive barring foreign nationals from Fable 5 and Mythos 5, prompting Anthropic to disable both models globally rather than enforce nationality-based access controls.

title: Microsoft discovers new lightweight backdoor that steals cryptocurrency
blurb: Microsoft disclosed Crypto Clipper, a self-propagating worm that spreads via .lnk files on USB drives and monitors clipboards for wallet addresses and seed phrases, exfiltrating stolen credentials and timed screenshots over Tor through a local SOCKS5 proxy. By bundling a portable Tor client and IP-less C2 with remote code execution, the malware doubles as a lightweight backdoor, so defenders should restrict USB autorun, block unauthorized Tor traffic, and monitor for clipboard-hijacking behavior.

title: Secret Network's Axelar Bridge Drained $4.67M via Infinite-Mint Flaw
blurb: Secret Network's Axelar bridge lost $4.67M after an attacker exploited a CW20-ICS20 contract that had two validation checks commented out, letting forged IBC packets mint unbacked wrapped tokens that were then redeemed for real escrowed assets. The flaw dated to the contract's March 2023 deployment and survived a March 2026 migration; Secret's default transaction encryption hid the depletion for seven days until a failed transfer surfaced it.

title: AryStinger Malware Infects 4,300 Legacy Routers to Build Reconnaissance Proxy Network
blurb: AryStinger malware has infected at least 4,300 legacy routers, exploiting CVE-2013-3307 in Linksys and CVE-2016-5681 in D-Link models to build a distributed reconnaissance and proxy network rather than a DDoS botnet. QiAnXin's XLab reports the pool is mostly D-Link DIR-850L devices, skewing to South Korea and China, with a second Go-based strain hitting QNAP NAS boxes via the patched CVE-2025-11837.

title: Fortinet: Oracle and other goliaths in firewall fallout as GPU 'democratization' blamed for mass hack
blurb: The "Fortibleed" credential-harvesting campaign compromised roughly 74,000 public-facing FortiGate devices, about half of all internet-exposed Fortinet firewalls, leaking enterprise sign-in details from Oracle, Comcast, Samsung, Foxconn, Siemens, PwC, Accenture, government agencies, and a NATO defense contractor. Attackers used a rented 45-GPU cluster to crack stolen passwords and generate credential variants at scale, so affected orgs should terminate all admin sessions and rotate every credential touched by exposed appliances.

title: Prompt Injection as Role Confusion
blurb: This writeup argues prompt injection is fundamentally a role-confusion problem: an LLM receives system prompts, user messages, tool outputs, and its own reasoning as one undifferentiated token stream, and the role tags meant to partition that stream are a weak signal the model can be made to misread. The authors use this framing to construct new attacks, explain mechanistic-interpretability findings, and predict when injections succeed, arguing that hardening role separation matters more than patching individual payloads.

title: 29-Year-Old Squid Proxy Bug 'Squidbleed' Can Leak Cleartext HTTP Requests
blurb: Squidbleed (CVE-2026-47729), a 29-year-old heap over-read in Squid's FTP directory-listing parser, lets any client already permitted to use the proxy leak another user's cleartext HTTP request, including Authorization headers and session tokens, by pointing the proxy at an attacker-controlled FTP server. The bug is live in Squid's default config and proof-of-concept code is public; patch and verify the guard lands in FtpGateway.cc rather than trusting version numbers, since distros ship their own backports.

title: Linux and Secure Boot certificate expiration (2025)
blurb: The Microsoft 2011 key used to sign the shim first-stage UEFI bootloader expires in September, after which Microsoft will stop signing shim with it, breaking Secure Boot installs of new Linux distributions on affected systems. A replacement key has existed since 2023 but may not be present in firmware and can require a vendor firmware update that may never ship. Defenders running Secure Boot fleets should audit firmware key databases now and push fwupd/LVFS updates before the cutover.

title: FortiBleed campaign used custom FortiGate sniffer to steal credentials
blurb: SOCRadar reports that the FortiBleed campaign has targeted more than 430,000 FortiGate firewalls since February 2026, deploying a Golang tool called FortigateSniffer that abuses the legitimate FortiOS "diagnose sniffer packet" command to harvest credentials from RADIUS, NTLM, Kerberos, and LDAP traffic. The initial-access broker behind it gains admin access via credential stuffing and brute-force, so defenders should rotate FortiGate credentials and audit SSH sessions for sniffer activity.

title: JaredFromSubway MEV bot hacked in $15 million crypto theft
blurb: The JaredFromSubway MEV bot lost $15M after an attacker deployed fake liquidity pools and tokens that appeared as profitable opportunities, tricking the bot's automated execution logic into granting ERC-20 spending approvals to attacker-controlled helper contracts. The attacker accumulated up to 92.16 WETH in unused allowances over staged test transactions, then drained WETH, USDC, and USDT via transferFrom; bounty offers of up to $7.5M for partial return have gone unanswered.

title: Following user outcry, AMD reinstates memory encryption in consumer CPUs
blurb: AMD will reinstate Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) on consumer Ryzen processors after silently stripping the full-memory encryption via firmware update, drawing widespread user backlash. The protection defends against cold boot and other physical-access attacks, and its quiet removal was undetectable on Windows and required significant technical effort to confirm on Linux.

title: GitHub Actions hardens checkout security to block ‘pwn request’ attacks
blurb: GitHub released actions/checkout v7 on June 18, which automatically fails workflows that fetch unreviewed fork PR code under pull_request_target or workflow_run triggers, closing the long-standing "pwn request" vector that leaked repository secrets. On July 16 the secure-by-default behavior backports to all supported major versions, so workflows pinned to floating tags like actions/checkout@v4 inherit the change automatically while SHA-pinned ones must upgrade via Dependabot.

title: WhatsApp phishing attack uses fake business docs to hack PCs
blurb: A WhatsApp campaign tracked by Kaspersky is spreading obfuscated VBScript files from compromised accounts, disguised as financial and billing documents to trick contacts into executing them. The chain disables UAC via Registry edits and silently installs the legitimate ManageEngine Endpoint Central RMM tool pointed at attacker servers, granting full remote administration across victims in Brazil, India, Mexico, the UK, and at least seven other countries.

title: OpenAI expands Daybreak with Patch the Planet and full GPT-5.5-Cyber release
blurb: OpenAI expanded its Daybreak program with Patch the Planet, an open-source patching initiative built with Trail of Bits and HackerOne, alongside an updated Codex Security plugin and the full release of GPT-5.5-Cyber. More than 30 projects including cURL, Python, and Go have signed on, with every AI-surfaced finding reviewed by a human security engineer before reaching maintainers.

title: Crypto Heist Fueled by Elaborate Fake Reputation-Boosting Campaign
blurb: Check Point uncovered a global cryptocurrency-theft campaign that builds fake credibility across GitHub, SourceForge, YouTube, and a WordPress phishing hub to push a Rust-based clipboard hijacker with Windows and macOS variants. The malware swaps copied wallet addresses for Bitcoin, Ethereum, Monero, Binance Chain, and Solana and maintains persistence, so defenders should flag clipboard-monitoring behavior and treat "crypto advantage" tools promoted via coordinated reviews as malicious.

title: Red-Teaming after Mythos — Zico Kolter & Matt Fredrikson, Gray Swan
blurb: In a Latent Space interview, Gray Swan cofounders Zico Kolter and Matt Fredrikson argue that AI security is a distinct discipline from traditional cybersecurity, because agents like Codex and Claude Code expose a new exploit class via indirect prompt injection. They detail Gray Swan's tooling, including Shade, an automated red-teaming model they say now outperforms humans at breaking AI systems, and contend that frontier models do not become inherently safer as they scale.

title: Flock-Powered Police Chiefs Stalking Women Shows Why Warrants Are Needed
blurb: The police chief of Holiday Hills, Illinois, was arrested June 18, 2026, and charged with two felony counts of official misconduct for using Prairie Grove's Flock license plate readers and the LEADS database to track six people, including three former romantic partners. The Institute for Justice has documented at least 18 similar cases of officers abusing Flock LPR, fueling a constitutional challenge that argues such tracking should require a warrant.

title: Security shops among the 'hundreds' of Klue hack victims
blurb: Attackers used a compromised legacy credential tied to a Klue integration service to mint OAuth tokens into customers' Salesforce instances, exfiltrating CRM data including business contacts, price quotes, and sales messaging from hundreds of the market-intelligence firm's customers. Security vendors including Huntress, Snyk, HackerOne, Tanium, and Recorded Future were among the victims, and the group Icarus claimed the June 11 breach, so affected orgs should revoke Klue's OAuth grants and audit Salesforce access logs.

title: Cloudflare teams up with big browsers to help websites tell welcome from unwelcome visitors
blurb: Cloudflare partnered with major browser vendors on Web Bot Auth, a standard that uses cryptographically signed HTTP message signatures to let sites verify which automated agents are legitimate rather than relying on spoofable user-agent strings or IP allowlists. Operators can register signing keys so welcome crawlers and AI agents authenticate themselves, giving defenders a cleaner signal to block unverified bot traffic without breaking sanctioned automation.

title: Cloudflare teams up with Chrome, Firefox, and Edge on a privacy-first anti-bot protocol
blurb: Cloudflare, Mozilla, Google, Microsoft, and Shopify are co-developing PACT (Private Access Control Tokens), a protocol that issues anonymous tokens proving a visitor is human or an authorized agent, replacing CAPTCHAs and forced logins without tracking users or reconstructing browsing history. The group plans to submit it for formal standardization as Cloudflare Radar data shows bots now generate roughly 58% of global HTTP requests, overtaking human traffic.

title: Trump executive orders speed up post-quantum migration, boost industry
blurb: Trump signed two executive orders Monday accelerating federal adoption of NIST-vetted post-quantum encryption ahead of the current 2035 deadline and directing federal financing toward the domestic quantum computing industry. Civilian agencies that miss the new migration deadline must report to OMB explaining why, following a May Commerce announcement of more than $2B in CHIPS Act incentives for nine quantum companies.

title: Tata Electronics, a major tech supplier to Apple and Tesla, confirms data breach
blurb: Tata Electronics, an Apple and Tesla supplier, confirmed a cybersecurity incident after a forum listing advertised more than 630GB of allegedly stolen data, including purported Apple supplier specifications, Tesla manufacturing documents, Outlook conversations, and SAP records across over 204,300 files. Tata says operations are unaffected but declined to confirm whether customer data was exposed; Reuters reports a ransom demand was made and that Apple is investigating.

title: President Trump signs two executive orders aimed at speeding the development of advanced quantum computers and mitigating the security threats they present (Amrith Ramkumar/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: Trump signed two executive orders directing federal agencies to accelerate quantum computer development while preparing defenses against the cryptographic threats those machines pose. The orders push faster migration to post-quantum cryptography across government systems, signaling that agencies and their vendors should prioritize PQC roadmaps now rather than waiting for cryptographically relevant quantum hardware to arrive.

title: Patch the Planet: a Daybreak initiative to support open source maintainers
blurb: OpenAI launched Patch the Planet, a Daybreak initiative built with Trail of Bits that pairs AI-assisted vulnerability research using OpenAI's models and Codex Security with human review to find and patch flaws in critical open-source projects. Initial participants include cURL, pyca/cryptography, aiohttp, Sigstore, NATS Server, and the Go and Python projects, with Trail of Bits, HackerOne, and Calif handling triage, patch development, and coordinated disclosure.

title: Never Give Them Your Face
blurb: The author argues that online age-verification laws are functionally identity-verification mandates: to confirm no minor is present, a service must check everyone, turning a rule written for teenagers into a universal ID checkpoint backed by government documents or facial scans. The piece notes that almost none of these systems accept a document attesting only to being over eighteen, because the captured data — name, birthdate, document number, face — is the point, and unlike a password, a face cannot be reset once breached.

title: Ukraine puts its Russian war trophies online for allies to pick apart
blurb: Ukraine published a public database of captured Russian military hardware, inviting allied governments and defense researchers to analyze the recovered electronics, weapons systems, and embedded components. The trove offers Western intelligence and supply-chain analysts a direct look at the foreign chips and commercial parts Russia relies on, exposing sanctions-evasion routes and the provenance of components inside fielded systems.

title: AWS Continuum offers devs help with securing code
blurb: AWS released Continuum, a service that continuously discovers, validates, and remediates vulnerabilities across first-party and third-party code, proposing fixes through existing dev workflows and adding STRIDE threat modeling generated from source or design docs. An "enforce mode" lets it autonomously patch code lapses once it learns an environment's guardrails, and it inherits penetration-testing and code-scanning capabilities from AWS Security Agent.

title: Court rules SAVE database illegal, orders it dismantled
blurb: A federal court ruled the Trump administration's SAVE national voter database illegal and ordered it dismantled, finding it violated the Privacy Act, the Social Security Act, and the Administrative Procedure Act. Judge Sparkle Sooknanan vacated the system after records showed agencies knowingly combined unreliable citizenship data from multiple databases, resulting in eligible voters being wrongly flagged as non-citizens and removed from the rolls.

title: Tata cyberattack allegedly exposes confidential Apple documents
blurb: Tata Electronics confirmed a cyberattack in which the ransomware group World Leaks exfiltrated over 630GB across more than 200,000 files, including component design specs for customers Apple and Tesla, employee passport copies, and years of email and event logs. The data has been on the dark web since at least June 10, Tata has received a ransom demand, and Apple is running a full analysis; manufacturing partners should treat shared design documents as exposed.

title: Breaking Into a Prison Tablet
blurb: Hardware hacker Hugh Jeffreys jailbroke a sealed US prison tablet, an Iview Optimus-C-8001 running a locked-down Windows 10 Home kiosk install, by hacksawing the opaque case open and bypassing the browser lockdown. With no drive encryption in place and the OS unpatched since 2018, full access was trivial once physical teardown exposed the device's own USB port.

title: Cloudflare forms pact with browser giants to protect your privacy with tokens
blurb: Cloudflare partnered with Mozilla, Google, and Microsoft on a proposed web standard called Private Access Tokens (PACT), which issues anonymous "personhood" tokens that browsers present to other sites as proof a visitor is human, without revealing identity or browsing history. The standard aims to replace cookie tracking and CAPTCHA as agentic AI traffic grows, distinguishing human users from autonomous agents acting on their behalf.

title: Sources: Meta internally exposed data from its employee-tracking program meant to help train its AI models, including full prompts and private conversations (Wired)
blurb: Meta internally exposed data from an employee-tracking program built to train its AI models, with full prompts and private conversations accessible to workers who shouldn't have had access, according to Wired. The incident adds to scrutiny of how the company sources and governs training data, and raises questions about access controls on the sensitive human-generated datasets feeding its models.

title: Anthropic’s new privacy policy collects biometric data from flagged Claude users
blurb: Anthropic updated its privacy policy, effective July 8, to require a flagged subset of Claude users to submit government IDs and selfies for identity verification, generating facial geometry templates that may qualify as biometric data under state laws. Verification is handled by Persona, a Thiel-backed firm previously dropped by Discord over ID-sharing concerns and tied to a reported exposure of roughly 2,500 files on a US government endpoint.

title: MEV Bot Exploited for $15M via Fake-Token Route Attack
blurb: An MEV bot lost $15M after an attacker baited its arbitrage logic into routing through a fake-token pool.

title: Intel agencies: Frontier AI models will reshape cybersecurity faster than expected
blurb: The Five Eyes intelligence alliance warned in a joint statement that frontier AI models capable of transforming offensive and defensive cyber operations are "months away" from broad public availability, citing models like Anthropic's Fable 5 and OpenAI's Daybreak. The agencies, with signatures from the NSA and acting CISA leadership, flagged legacy systems, slow patching cycles, and weak identity controls as the weaknesses AI will excel at exploiting.

title: Cisco's SSE Rollout Shows The Real Work Behind Zero Trust
blurb: Cisco IT engineers detailed their 12-month migration from a hardware-heavy, 12-location VPN model to a unified cloud-delivered Security Service Edge (SSE) fabric built on their earlier Zero Trust Access foundation. They report an 18% drop in help desk cases, near-zero security incident rates, and the retirement of 20+ legacy VPN options. The writeup focuses on the engineering grind of dismantling end-of-life infrastructure and consolidating disparate hardware to cut MTTR and close visibility gaps.

title: Free VPN And Streaming Apps Are Polluting Business Networks With Residential Proxy Traffic
blurb: Infoblox Threat Intel found that 65% of its Threat Defense Cloud customers made DNS queries to residential-proxy domains in 2026, totaling over 500 billion queries per month. Free VPN, streaming, and productivity apps often enroll users' devices as exit nodes via buried EULA clauses, lending home routers, phones, and IoT gear to fraud, scraping, and other abuse. Defenders should monitor DNS for proxy-orchestration domains and treat affected hosts as exposed to legal and IP-reputation fallout.

title: Announcing Amazon EC2 G7 instances accelerated by NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs
blurb: AWS released its EC2 G7 instances into general availability, becoming the first major cloud provider to offer NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs paired with custom sixth-gen Intel Xeon processors. The instances deliver up to 4.6x AI inference and 2.1x graphics performance over G6, with 32GB of memory per GPU, 700 Gbps of EFA networking, and up to eight GPUs per node for inference, rendering, and VDI workloads.

title: Microsoft fixes AutoGen Studio flaw that enabled code execution
blurb: Microsoft patched a vulnerability chain dubbed AutoJack in AutoGen Studio, the GUI for its open-source multi-agent framework, which could let a malicious webpage trick a developer's browsing agent into executing arbitrary PowerShell or Bash commands on the host. The flaw was fixed before any PyPI release, so only developers who built from the main GitHub branch during a narrow window were exposed.

title: A new unpatchable flaw in Apple chips opens the door to an iPhone jailbreak
blurb: Paradigm Shift, a Barcelona-based offensive security firm, published "usbliter8," a proof-of-concept exploit for an unpatchable Boot ROM flaw in Apple's A12 and A13 chips that affects the iPhone XS, XR, and 11. Because the flaw sits in immutable silicon, no software update can fix it, leaving hardware refresh the only mitigation for older fleets and handing forensics vendors like Cellebrite another physical-access path into seized devices.

title: ShapedPlugin WordPress Pro Plugins Backdoored in Supply Chain Attack
blurb: Attackers compromised ShapedPlugin's build and distribution pipeline, injecting backdoor code into Pro releases of Product Slider Pro for WooCommerce, Real Testimonials Pro, and Smart Post Show Pro delivered through the vendor's licensed update channels, with one flaw rated CVSS 10.0. The malware harvests wp-config.php credentials, admin accounts, SMTP secrets, and recent WooCommerce order data, making this a procurement-level reminder to audit third-party plugin supply chains, not just core platforms.

title: FFmpeg fixes PixelSmash flaw in widely used video decoder
blurb: FFmpeg patched PixelSmash (CVE-2026-8461), a high-severity heap out-of-bounds write in the MagicYUV decoder that JFrog used to achieve remote code execution on a Jellyfin media server through its normal library-scan pipeline. Any application built on libavcodec is exposed, including Kodi, OBS Studio, Nextcloud, PhotoPrism, and Linux desktop thumbnail generators, so IT teams should prioritize updating FFmpeg across self-hosted media and file-preview infrastructure.

title: AryStinger Malware Infects 4,300 Legacy Routers to Build Reconnaissance Proxy Network
blurb: AryStinger malware has infected at least 4,300 legacy routers, mostly D-Link DIR-850L units running Realtek RTL819X chips, turning them into a distributed reconnaissance and proxy network that scans, fingerprints, and tunnels traffic before an attacker breaks in. A second strain hits QNAP NAS boxes through CVE-2025-11837, a flaw patched in November 2025, so unpatched appliances and end-of-life routers remain the exposed surface IT teams need to retire or segment.

title: Nearly half of LG smart TV apps contain residential proxy SDKs
blurb: Security firm Spur scanned 6,038 LG and Samsung smart TV apps and found residential proxy SDKs, including Bright Data, embedded in 2,058 of them, quietly routing third-party internet traffic through users' home networks. For IT teams, internet-connected TVs sitting on corporate and remote-work networks now represent unmanaged proxy hosts that route outside traffic through trusted IP space, expanding the attack surface most endpoint and network policies never account for.

title: Fortinet: Oracle and other goliaths in firewall fallout as GPU 'democratization' blamed for mass hack
blurb: Attackers compromised roughly 74,000 internet-facing FortiGate devices, nearly half of all publicly exposed Fortinet firewalls, harvesting enterprise credentials from organizations including Oracle, Samsung, Siemens, PwC, Accenture, and a NATO defense contractor. Researchers tied the campaign to a rented 45-GPU cluster used to crack and generate password variants at scale, evidence that cheap on-demand GPU access has lowered the bar for credential attacks on enterprise edge infrastructure.

title: FortiBleed campaign used custom FortiGate sniffer to steal credentials
blurb: SOCRadar reported that the FortiBleed campaign has compromised FortiGate firewalls since at least February 2026 using "FortigateSniffer," a Golang tool that abuses FortiOS's built-in diagnose sniffer command to harvest RADIUS, NTLM, Kerberos, and LDAP credentials across 24 protocols. The finding contradicts Fortinet's claim that the incident is only a collection of old leaked credentials, indicating an active operation against more than 430,000 internet-exposed devices.

title: Linux and Secure Boot certificate expiration (2025)
blurb: Microsoft's 2011 key used to sign the shim UEFI bootloader for Secure Boot expires in September, after which it will no longer sign the first-stage loader that Linux distributions rely on to boot under Secure Boot. A replacement key has existed since 2023 but is absent from many systems and may require a vendor firmware update, leaving distributors and IT teams to handle new installs and patch coverage before the deadline.

title: 29-Year-Old Squid Proxy Bug 'Squidbleed' Can Leak Cleartext HTTP Requests
blurb: Researchers at Calif.io disclosed Squidbleed (CVE-2026-47729), a 29-year-old heap over-read in the Squid web proxy that lets any trusted client leak another user's cleartext HTTP requests, including credentials and session tokens. The flaw is live in Squid's default config and hits shared deployments like schools, offices, and public Wi-Fi; proof-of-concept code is public, though no in-the-wild exploitation has been reported.

title: AWS enters the context layer race with a graph that learns from agents, not manual curation
blurb: AWS announced a context intelligence stack for AI agents, centered on AWS Context, a knowledge graph service that infers data relationships automatically and refines itself based on how agents use it, alongside GA of S3 Annotations and a preview of skill assets in Glue Data Catalog. Metadata publishes in open Apache Iceberg format with IAM and Lake Formation permissions inherited per query, positioning AWS against context-layer rivals on existing enterprise controls.

title: Following user outcry, AMD reinstates memory encryption in consumer CPUs
blurb: AMD reversed course and will restore Transparent Secure Memory Encryption (TSME) to consumer Ryzen chips via firmware, after a recent update silently stripped the cold-boot-attack protection from lower-end processors. The episode is a reminder that platform security features delivered through firmware can be revoked at the vendor's discretion, which complicates endpoint hardening assumptions for IT teams standardized on AMD's consumer-tier hardware.

title: Production-Ready Autonomous Incident Resolution with AWS DevOps Agent (now GA) and Datadog MCP Server
blurb: AWS made its DevOps Agent generally available alongside the now-GA Datadog MCP Server, giving teams a production-ready path to autonomous incident triage and resolution across AWS, multicloud, and on-premises environments. The integration lets AI agents pull Datadog observability data through the Model Context Protocol instead of brittle API calls, positioning AWS and Datadog as the joint stack for automated incident response and cutting on-call resolution from hours to minutes.

title: WhatsApp phishing attack uses fake business docs to hack PCs
blurb: A WhatsApp phishing campaign tracked by Kaspersky is spreading obfuscated VBScript files disguised as financial documents, hijacking compromised accounts to reach victims' contacts across Brazil, India, Mexico, the UK, and seven other countries. The infection chain disables UAC and silently installs ManageEngine Endpoint Central, a legitimate IT management tool, configured to phone attacker-controlled servers and hand over full remote administration of the Windows machine.

title: Security shops among the 'hundreds' of Klue hack victims
blurb: A breach at competitive-intelligence vendor Klue exposed data across hundreds of customer organizations, with cybersecurity firms among the victims. The incident is a reminder that third-party SaaS tools sit inside the enterprise trust boundary, so IT leaders should treat their CI and sales-enablement vendors as in-scope for vendor-risk review and incident-response planning, not as low-priority point solutions.

title: Boost BigQuery with Python: Managed Python UDFs now generally available
blurb: Google made BigQuery Managed Python UDFs generally available, letting data teams run custom Python with libraries like NumPy, pandas, and scikit-learn directly inside SQL queries on serverless, auto-scaling infrastructure. The functions are now fully supported for production workloads and integrated into BigQuery's billing SKUs, removing the need to maintain separate containers or compute services to run Python helper logic against enterprise data.

title: Cloudflare teams up with big browsers to help websites tell welcome from unwelcome visitors
blurb: Cloudflare partnered with major browser vendors on a standard that lets websites cryptographically verify which automated agents and crawlers are legitimate, separating sanctioned AI bots from scrapers and abusive traffic. For enterprise IT teams running bot management and WAF policies, a browser-backed signing scheme could replace brittle user-agent and IP heuristics with verifiable identity, reshaping how access controls are written across the AI-agent traffic now hitting corporate sites.

title: Microsoft and Chevron plan one of the largest gas-powered data center projects in US
blurb: Microsoft and Chevron will develop Project Kilby, a 2.67-gigawatt natural gas plant in West Texas dedicated to a Microsoft-operated AI and cloud data center under a 20-year power purchase agreement. The dedicated buildout signals that Microsoft is prioritizing guaranteed AI compute capacity over its 2030 carbon-neutral pledge, since the plant could emit more than 13 million tons of CO2 annually.

title: Trump executive orders speed up post-quantum migration, boost industry
blurb: Trump signed two executive orders Monday accelerating federal adoption of post-quantum encryption ahead of the current 2035 deadline and directing federal financing toward the domestic quantum computing industry. Civilian agencies that miss the new timeline must justify the delay to OMB, which pulls forward the timeline for vendors selling NIST-vetted quantum-resistant crypto to the government and pressures enterprise stacks to plan their own migrations sooner.

title: Introducing the Cloudflare One stack: agent-powered deployment
blurb: Cloudflare released the Cloudflare One stack, a set of agent skills that configure, deploy, and manage a customer's Zero Trust environment and map an existing security suite into Cloudflare One. The toolkit packages Cloudflare's SASE migration expertise into prescriptive guidance any agent can execute, reducing the manual work of decoding network topology and policy intent during a Zero Trust rollout.

title: Oracle Cut 21,000 Jobs in 12 Months, Says AI Replaced Some Roles
blurb: Oracle cut 21,000 jobs over the past 12 months, a deeper reduction than previously disclosed, attributing some of the eliminated roles to AI taking over the work. The cuts land as Oracle pours capital into AI infrastructure, signaling a shift toward funding data-center buildout over headcount as it leans on automation to hold down operating costs.

title: Dell launches AI server based on Nvidia Vera Rubin GPUs
blurb: Dell launched the PowerEdge XE8812, a liquid-cooled AI server built on Nvidia's Vera Rubin NVL4 architecture that scales to 144 GPUs per rack and anchors the Dell AI Factory with Nvidia preconfigured stack of compute, PowerScale and PowerStore storage, networking, and Nvidia AI Enterprise software. The system adds 50% more memory per socket and GPU versus the prior GB200 generation, letting enterprises run larger models and simulations entirely in-memory without staging or swapping.

title: Blinding the Watchmen: Abusing Cloud Logging Services for Defense Evasion and Visibility
blurb: Unit 42 documented attack techniques that target cloud logging services like AWS CloudTrail and Google Cloud Logging, showing how attackers disrupt log flows to evade detection or quietly route copies of audit logs into their own accounts for continuous visibility. The breakdown gives security and cloud-ops teams concrete misconfigurations to harden and specific service-misuse patterns to add to their detection rules.

title: Arm servers capture over 45% of data center market revenue — GPU clusters and high-end AI infrastructure fuel a tectonic shift away from x86
blurb: Arm-based servers now command over 45% of data center revenue and accelerated GPU/ASIC/FPGA systems drove more than 70% of a record $122.6B in Q1 2026 server sales, per IDC, with Dell's revenue surging 244% to $20.3B on AI demand. Enterprise buyers standardizing on x86 should plan for an architecture split, as sovereign AI and branded-vendor deployments increasingly steer infrastructure procurement toward Arm and accelerator-heavy systems.

title: The memory crisis is getting so bad that even retro RAM prices are going to the Moon
blurb: Memory prices are climbing across the board as AI infrastructure demand soaks up DRAM and NAND supply, with the squeeze now reaching even legacy and retro RAM formats that normally sit outside the data center supply chain. Enterprise buyers face higher costs and longer lead times on server memory and storage refreshes, pushing IT teams to lock in procurement early or stretch existing hardware further into 2026.

title: President Trump signs two executive orders aimed at speeding the development of advanced quantum computers and mitigating the security threats they present (Amrith Ramkumar/Wall Street Journal)
blurb: President Trump signed two executive orders directing federal agencies to accelerate development of advanced quantum computers and to harden government systems against the encryption-breaking threat such machines will eventually pose. The orders push agencies toward post-quantum cryptography migration timelines, signaling enterprise IT teams that NIST-standard PQC adoption is moving from optional to mandated across the federal supply chain.

title: Apple's A12 and A13 Chips Facing New Unpatchable Exploit
blurb: Researchers published usbliter8, an unpatchable BootROM exploit affecting Apple's A12 and A13 chips, covering iPhone XS through iPhone 11.

title: Microsoft says Windows 11 26H2 is coming soon, details upgrade process
blurb: Microsoft confirmed Windows 11 26H2 as its next annual feature update and detailed a shared servicing model that lets devices on 24H2 and 25H2 upgrade through a 174 KB enablement package and a restart, versus a 6.5 GB full install. Systems on 23H2 and older sit on a different servicing branch and need the full upgrade, with no changes to hardware requirements and general availability expected in the fall.

title: Tata Electronics, a major tech supplier to Apple and Tesla, confirms data breach
blurb: Tata Electronics, a key Apple and Tesla supplier in India's semiconductor push, confirmed a cybersecurity incident after a hacker forum listed more than 630GB of allegedly stolen files, including purported Apple supplier specs and Tesla manufacturing documents. Reuters reported a ransom demand and that Apple is investigating, raising third-party risk concerns for OEMs that shifted production to India to diversify supply chains beyond China.

title: The systemd 261 release brings a software TPM, new OS installer
blurb: systemd 261 shipped with an IMDS subsystem that exposes cloud instance metadata through a local Varlink API, recognizing providers including AWS EC2, Azure, Google Compute Engine, and Oracle Cloud, plus a software TPM service built on IBM's swtpm for hardware that lacks a physical one. The release also adds state persistence across kexec reboots, a textual OS installer in systemd-sysinstall, and graduates systemd-sysupdate out of experimental status.

title: Filing: Oracle's global workforce fell by 21,000 employees in the past 12 months to 141,000 as of May 31; the company says AI adoption has led to reductions (Brody Ford/Bloomberg)
blurb: Oracle's global headcount fell by 21,000 over the past 12 months to 141,000 as of May 31, with the company attributing the reductions to AI adoption in a recent filing. The cuts land as Oracle ramps capital spending on AI data center capacity, signaling a bet that automation can offset payroll while infrastructure costs climb.

title: AWS Continuum offers devs help with securing code
blurb: AWS launched Continuum, a service that continuously discovers, investigates, and remediates code vulnerabilities across first-party and third-party software, with an enforce mode that autonomously fixes lapses once it learns an environment's guardrails. The service pulls penetration testing and code scanning from AWS's existing Security Agent and adds threat modeling that generates STRIDE-format models from source or design docs, positioning AWS to keep security triage pace with agentic development.

title: Texas lassoes massive Microsoft datacenter - and 20 years of gas turbine emissions
blurb: Texas regulators approved a large Microsoft datacenter backed by on-site gas turbines cleared to emit for the next 20 years, sidestepping grid constraints by generating power directly. The deal underscores how AI infrastructure demand is pushing hyperscalers toward dedicated fossil-fuel generation, complicating the carbon-neutral commitments enterprises increasingly weigh when selecting a cloud provider.

title: Court rules SAVE database illegal, orders it dismantled
blurb: A federal court ruled that the Trump administration's SAVE national voter database violates the Privacy Act, Social Security Act, and Administrative Procedure Act, and ordered it dismantled after agencies "haphazardly" combined citizenship data from multiple government systems they knew was unreliable. The ruling, brought by the League of Women Voters and EPIC, underscores the legal exposure agencies face when repurposing personal data across databases without congressional authorization or required notice-and-comment procedures.

title: Tata cyberattack allegedly exposes confidential Apple documents
blurb: Tata Electronics confirmed a cyberattack in which the group World Leaks claims to have stolen over 630GB of data, including component design and quality-inspection documents tied to customers Apple and Tesla, plus employee passport copies. The breach lands on a key Apple manufacturing partner and underscores third-party supply-chain exposure for enterprises whose proprietary specs and IP sit on a contractor's systems beyond their direct security control.

title: Cloudflare forms pact with browser giants to protect your privacy with tokens
blurb: Cloudflare is partnering with Google, Microsoft, and Mozilla on a new web standard called Private Access Control Tokens (PACT), which lets browsers present anonymous proof of "personhood" to distinguish human users from AI agents without cookies or CAPTCHAs. The standard aims to cut bot-mitigation friction as agentic traffic scales, and positions Cloudflare alongside the major browser vendors to shape how enterprises handle agent identity and abuse prevention.

title: Nvidia unveils Vera Rubin platform targeting AI, HPC infrastructure
blurb: Nvidia launched the Vera Rubin platform at ISC High Performance 2026, a rack-scale system pairing Vera CPUs and Rubin GPUs over NVLink-C2C with up to 144 GPUs, claiming over seven exaflops of AI performance and five petaflops of FP64 compute per rack. The 2.8x memory-bandwidth gain over Blackwell targets HPC buyers running climate, fluid-dynamics, and quantum-chemistry workloads who now consolidate simulation and AI training on one liquid-cooled infrastructure.

title: Nvidia says its AI data center design runs hotter to use a lot less water
blurb: Nvidia claims its Rubin-generation reference design for fully liquid-cooled data centers cuts water use from roughly 2.6 million gallons per megawatt per year to near zero by capturing heat at the chip and running servers as hot as 113°F. The pitch targets the water-and-power scrutiny dogging AI buildouts, but Nvidia's blog omits the capital cost of liquid versus air cooling and sidesteps construction and power-generation concerns.

title: Nvidia tops Ethernet data center switch charts by revenue as sales soar
blurb: Nvidia became the top data center Ethernet switch vendor by revenue in Q1 2026, with sales up 192.7% year over year to $2.1B and a 21.5% share, on demand for its Spectrum-X platform from hyperscalers building AI clusters. The win positions Nvidia's integrated GPU-plus-networking stack against incumbents Cisco, Broadcom, and Arista, who now face a buyer increasingly able to bundle compute and interconnect in a single procurement.

title: Troubleshoot formula errors quickly with Gemini in Google Sheets
blurb: Google added Gemini-powered formula error troubleshooting to Google Sheets, surfacing explanations and fixes for broken formulas directly in the spreadsheet. The feature is on by default where Gemini for Workspace in Sheets is enabled, covering Business and Enterprise Standard and Plus tiers, with a gradual rollout running up to 15 days from June 22.

title: AWS Weekly Roundup: NY Summit recap, Local Zone in Hanoi, Grok 4.3 in Bedrock, price reductions, and more (June 22, 2026)
blurb: At its NYC Summit, AWS introduced a stack of agent-focused launches, headlined by Continuum, an AI-native security service that scans and remediates code vulnerabilities, and a GA release of Bedrock AgentCore for moving agents into production. The weekly roundup also added Grok 4.3 to Bedrock, opened a Local Zone in Hanoi for Vietnamese data-residency needs, and posted unspecified price reductions across services.

title: Tesla plans to sell modular AI data center hardware called ‘Megapod'
blurb: Tesla filed an intent-to-use trademark for "Megapod," a modular, self-contained AI data center system bundling servers, networking, power distribution, and cooling, plus management software, less than a year after it killed its Dojo training computer. The product would compete directly with Nvidia's GB200 NVL72 and DGX SuperPOD, the rack-scale reference designs that Dell and Supermicro already build on, putting Tesla in a market Nvidia's chips effectively underpin.

title: Anthropic's Mythos mess just keeps getting more complicated
blurb: Anthropic disabled its Mythos 5 and Fable 5 models for all customers after the Trump administration issued an export-control order last week barring any foreign national, including Anthropic staff, from using them on national-security grounds. The Register's Kettle podcast digs into Amazon's possible role in the decision and warns that open-weight models will likely match the same capabilities, making the restriction hard to enforce for enterprise buyers caught without access.

title: The new database world according to Google: Inexact queries and AI in everything
blurb: Google laid out a database strategy built on inexact, semantic queries and AI embedded across its data platforms, with a product exec predicting that humans will no longer directly operate data platforms within three to five years. The pitch points enterprise buyers toward agent-driven querying over BigQuery and AlloyDB, reframing the database from a tool analysts run to infrastructure that AI agents interrogate on their behalf.

title: Windows 11 is getting Copilot on Microsoft 365 Business accounts again, unless you’re in Europe
blurb: Microsoft is reinstalling Copilot on commercial Microsoft 365 desktop apps via the Office update mechanism, a mid-June to mid-July rollout that admins can only block through scattered Admin Center and per-app settings rather than the Store auto-install toggle. EU users are exempt, likely due to competition law, so IT teams outside Europe face deliberate removal friction across Word, Excel, PowerPoint, Outlook, and Teams.

title: Microsoft and Chevron Sign 20-Year Power Deal For Texas Data Center
blurb: Microsoft signed a 20-year deal with Chevron for natural-gas-fired power to supply a proposed West Texas data center that could rank among the largest in the US. The agreement locks in long-term generation capacity outside the public grid, signaling that hyperscalers will increasingly contract directly with energy producers to secure power for AI buildouts rather than wait on utility interconnection.

title: Threat Brief: Mitigating Large-Scale Credential Attacks
blurb: Unit 42 details a large-scale password spraying campaign ("FortiBleed") harvesting credentials from internet-exposed Fortinet, Sophos, and MSSQL devices.

title: Announcing Amazon EC2 G7 instances accelerated by NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs
blurb: AWS made general availability for EC2 G7 instances, the first major cloud offering of NVIDIA RTX PRO 4500 Blackwell Server Edition GPUs, delivering up to 4.6x AI inference and 2.1x graphics performance over G6. Each instance scales to 8 GPUs and 256GB of GPU memory, with 700 Gbps of EFA networking (7x G6) and 7.6TB of local NVMe to keep large models and datasets close to compute.

title: Run isolated sandboxes with full lifecycle control: AWS Lambda introduces MicroVMs
blurb: AWS introduced Lambda MicroVMs, a Firecracker-backed serverless primitive that hands each user or session its own isolated, stateful execution environment with VM-level isolation, near-instant launch and resume, and pause-to-idle pricing. It targets multi-tenant apps that run untrusted user- or AI-generated code, such as coding assistants and analytics platforms, removing the choice between slow-booting VMs and containers that need custom kernel hardening.

title: How we found a bug in the hyper HTTP library
blurb: Cloudflare engineers spent six weeks tracing why their rearchitected Images binding intermittently returned truncated payloads, 200-status responses that cut a two-megabyte image down to a few hundred kilobytes, only for larger files. The culprit was a race condition in the hyper Rust HTTP library governing how processed image data streamed back to the client. The fix was four lines of code, a reminder that edge-network rollouts surface failure modes invisible under normal load.

title: Introducing the Cloudflare One stack: agent-powered deployment
blurb: Cloudflare released the Cloudflare One stack, a set of agent skills that configure, deploy, and manage a Zero Trust environment and map an existing security suite into Cloudflare One. The toolkit packages migration expertise from thousands of customer deployments, giving agents the network-topology and vendor-configuration context they otherwise lack to operate on SASE infrastructure.

title: The systemd 261 release brings a software TPM, new OS installer
blurb: systemd 261 shipped with an IMDS subsystem that exposes a local Varlink API for cloud instance metadata, recognizing EC2, Azure, GCE, Hetzner, and Oracle Cloud by their SMBIOS data. The release also adds kexec state preservation via FileDescriptorStorePreserve, a software TPM service backed by IBM's swtpm, and systemd-sysinstall, a Varlink-based textual OS installer.

title: Making secret scanning more trustworthy: Reducing false positives at scale
blurb: GitHub partnered with Microsoft Security & AI's Agents Offense team to add LLM-based contextual verification to its secret scanning, reasoning about how a detected value appears in code rather than whether it merely matches a secret-like pattern. The goal is to bring AI-detected secrets closer to the precision GitHub already achieves on provider-pattern detections across billions of pushes. Fewer low-value alerts means less triage toil and more developer trust in the system, without sacrificing coverage.

title: One vulnerability view: From scanner coverage to AI governance
blurb: GitLab 19.1 adds a single vulnerability view that enforces any SARIF-emitting third-party scanner across every project under one policy, eliminating the per-project drift that lets new repos ship unscanned for weeks. Third-party findings now flow through GitLab Duo's auto-remediation, with SAST false-positive triage and agentic resolution opening ready-to-merge fixes, plus a beta of AI audit event streaming to track agent behavior.

title: Linux and Secure Boot certificate expiration (2025)
blurb: The Microsoft 2011 key used to sign the shim first-stage UEFI bootloader expires in September, after which Microsoft will stop signing the bootloader that Linux distributions rely on to boot under Secure Boot. A 2023 replacement key exists but is missing from many systems and often requires a vendor firmware update, so the breakage mainly hits new installs on Secure Boot hardware and adds work for distributors and users.

title: GitHub Actions hardens checkout security to block ‘pwn request’ attacks
blurb: GitHub released actions/checkout v7, which now fails workflows that fetch unreviewed fork code under pull_request_target or workflow_run triggers, closing the "pwn request" hole that let attackers run forked code with full workflow privileges and reach repo secrets. The secure-by-default change backports to all supported major versions on July 16, so floating tags like actions/checkout@v4 pick it up automatically while SHA-pinned workflows need a manual bump or Dependabot upgrade.

title: Blinding the Watchmen: Abusing Cloud Logging Services for Defense Evasion and Visibility
blurb: Unit 42 researchers detail how attackers target cloud logging services like AWS CloudTrail and Google Cloud Logging to blind defenders, breaking down two attack classes: defense evasion that modifies or disables logging resources to execute unnoticed, and continuous visibility that siphons logs into attacker-controlled accounts. The writeup maps the specific configurations and detections platform teams need to catch logging-service tampering before it erases the audit trail you rely on for incident response.

title: AWS Weekly Roundup: NY Summit recap, Local Zone in Hanoi, Grok 4.3 in Bedrock, price reductions, and more (June 22, 2026)
blurb: AWS recapped its NY Summit, where it introduced Continuum, an AI-native security service for the code vulnerability lifecycle, pushed Bedrock AgentCore to GA with a managed orchestration harness and the new Context service, and added release-gating to its DevOps Agent. The roundup also covers a new Local Zone in Hanoi with S3 and EBS local snapshots for data residency, Grok 4.3 landing in Bedrock, and several price reductions.

title: Meta buys stake in Cred, appoints founder to lead WhatsApp
blurb: Meta invested $900M for a 20% stake in India-based fintech Cred and named founder Kunal Shah to lead WhatsApp, succeeding Will Cathcart. The move signals deeper commerce and payments ambitions for WhatsApp in India, its largest market by users, where the platform is racing to convert scale into monetizable transactions.

title: Sources: marketing tech startup AppsFlyer raised a $1B Series E at a $2.7B post-money valuation; Moloco, Google, Meta, and Unity acquire minority stakes (Kerry Flynn/Axios)
blurb: AppsFlyer raised a $1B Series E at a $2.7B post-money valuation, with Moloco, Google, Meta, and Unity all taking minority stakes in the mobile attribution and marketing measurement firm. The strategic investment from the largest ad platforms signals a bet on independent measurement as signal loss from privacy changes keeps complicating cross-channel attribution for app marketers.

title: Lighthouse agentic browsing scoring
blurb: Chrome's Lighthouse added an Agentic Browsing category that runs deterministic audits on how well a site supports machine interaction, checking WebMCP tool registration and accessibility-tree integrity and reporting a fractional pass ratio rather than the usual 0-100 score. Marketers tracking AI-search and agent traffic now have a CI-friendly signal for whether their pages are structured for agents to read and act on, alongside the SEO checks they already run.

title: LinkedIn lets users set core brand rules
blurb: LinkedIn is rolling out brand kits in Campaign Manager, letting marketers set color palettes, fonts, and a brand voice that its AI tools then apply to ad and content generation. LinkedIn auto-assembles a starting brand voice from a company's existing page and past posts, aiming to cut off-brand mistakes and manual rework in AI-drafted ads.

title: Adobe Just Made its Biggest AI Push Yet, and it Stretches from Photoshop to Disney World
blurb: Adobe rolled out five AI announcements in three days, led by a Firefly AI Assistant now in public beta inside Photoshop, Premiere, Illustrator, InDesign, and Frame.io, plus a Firefly Foundry deal training custom models on Disney Imagineering's own catalogue. The strategy is to become the creative and marketing AI layer spanning solo Photoshop users to enterprise brands, with IP-clean models pitched as the wedge against scraped-data rivals like Canva and Figma.

title: Snapchat launches ‘House of the Dragon’ AR ad in New York City
blurb: Snapchat launched an interactive AR ad for HBO's "House of the Dragon" in Times Square, letting people project themselves onto large-format billboards and insert themselves into scenes from the show. The activation pairs Snapchat's AR tech with high-traffic out-of-home placement, giving brands a templated way to turn static billboards into shareable, camera-driven moments that extend reach into social.

title: Snapchat launches AI-powered ad creation tools
blurb: Snapchat released a suite of AI-powered ad creation tools, including a chatbot-guided campaign setup and the Snap Creator Network that matches advertisers with relevant creator partners. The setup assistant lowers the barrier for smaller advertisers running Snap campaigns, while the creator-matching layer is Snap's bid to capture more influencer spend that currently flows to TikTok and Instagram.

title: Kids’ Online Safety Bill Deal Struck by House GOP, Democrats
blurb: House lawmakers reached a bipartisan deal on a legislative package to strengthen online safety and privacy protections for children and teens, advancing a long-stalled policy priority. Brands targeting younger audiences should expect tighter rules on data collection, ad targeting, and default privacy settings for minors, with compliance demands likely landing on platforms and advertisers alike.

title: WSJ: $1.9M in Fake Bets Propped Up Polymarket Creator Videos
blurb: A Wall Street Journal investigation found that roughly $1.9M in bets shown across 1,105 Polymarket-promoting creator videos were fake, staged on dummy versions of the platform and produced by paid clippers who were told not to disclose the arrangement. The fabricated-engagement campaign targeted U.S. audiences as Polymarket pursues regulatory approval and institutional backing, a reminder that influencer-driven proof can be manufactured wholesale.

title: AppsFlyer Reportedly Lands $1B At $2.7B Valuation To Help Companies Track Digital Ads
blurb: AppsFlyer raised more than $1B in a Series E at a $2.7B post-money valuation, with Moloco, Google, Meta, and Unity each taking minority stakes in the mobile attribution platform. The investor list signals that as AI automates ad buying, marketers increasingly want independent measurement of return on ad spend rather than attribution numbers self-reported by the same platforms selling the inventory.

title: Better search, worse web
blurb: Google reports that AI Overviews now reach over a billion people and have driven a double-digit rise in search usage, but the author argues that every gain in answer quality removes a reason for users to click through to the sites that supplied the answers. DuckDuckGo installs jumped roughly 30% after Google's 2026 I/O, a signal that some users want a search they can switch off.

title: Instagram expands to Samsung Smart TV
blurb: Instagram is now available on the majority of connected TV devices in the U.S. through a new Samsung Smart TV app and has begun testing viewing features built for the big screen. The move pushes Reels and creator content into living-room ad inventory, giving brands a path to reach Instagram audiences on CTV alongside their existing Meta buys.

title: What is a Good Organic CTR? Real Website Benchmarks (June 2026)
blurb: Whole-site organic CTR now sits between 1% and 2% for most sites, based on aggregated Google Search Console data from 422,421 websites, with the median running well below the position-1 figures most teams cite. CTR is falling fastest in answer-heavy niches: Pets & Animals (0.80%) and Health (1.12%) trigger AI Overviews on 37% to 43% of queries. The takeaway for content teams is to shift effort away from informational keywords AI now answers and toward intent and formats Overviews rarely intercept.

title: Americans and AI 2026: Chatbots, Smart Devices, and Views on Impact
blurb: Pew's 2026 survey of 5,119 US adults found that about half now use AI chatbots, up sharply from summer 2024, with roughly one in four using them daily and search and work as the top use cases. Sentiment runs negative, including among younger adults, as more respondents expect AI to harm rather than help society and majorities say it is advancing too fast and threatens their personal data.

title: What happens after coding is solved? | Fiona Fung (Manager of the Claude Code and Cowork Teams)
blurb: Fiona Fung, who leads Anthropic's Claude Code and Cowork teams, argues that when AI handles most of the coding, the engineering manager's job shifts from coordinating output to deciding what's worth building and keeping context across parallel agent work. She reports Anthropic engineers now ship roughly 8x more code per quarter than in 2021, and names context-switching across concurrent AI tasks as the unsolved bottleneck no tooling has cracked yet.

title: Microsoft discovers new lightweight backdoor that steals cryptocurrency
blurb: Microsoft detected a self-propagating worm it calls Crypto Clipper that spreads via USB .lnk files and scans clipboards for wallet addresses and seed phrases, exfiltrating credentials and screenshots to attacker servers over a portable Tor client routed through a local SOCKS5 proxy. The malware blends data theft with remote code execution, functioning as a lightweight backdoor without traditional installers or exposed IP-based command-and-control infrastructure.

title: NYSE Parent ICE and OKX Form 50-50 Joint Venture for Tokenized Equities, Co-Chaired by Andrew Cuomo
blurb: Intercontinental Exchange, the parent of the NYSE, and crypto exchange OKX are forming a 50-50 joint venture to bring NYSE-listed equities on-chain, with former New York Governor Andrew Cuomo co-chairing. Pending regulatory approval, the venture will operate as a US-registered broker-dealer and futures commission merchant, building on ICE's March investment that valued OKX at $25B.

title: Jaredfromsubway.eth, Ethereum's Most Active Sandwich Bot, Drained for $7.5M Over the Weekend
blurb: An attacker drained more than $7.5M from jaredfromsubway.eth, the Ethereum address responsible for roughly 70% of all sandwich attacks on the network between late 2024 and October 2025, in what Blockaid identified as a counter-MEV honeypot rather than a phishing or smart-contract exploit. It is the largest single-event loss publicly tied to an MEV operator to date, with some of the stolen WETH, USDC, and USDT already routed through Tornado Cash.

title: Ethereum Foundation’s Interim ED Lays Out Execution Plan: MEV Elimination, Default Privacy, ETH Pay
blurb: The Ethereum Foundation's interim Executive Director, Bastian Aue, published a six-part execution thread committing the EF to treating toxic MEV extraction as core protocol work, making privacy a network default, and shifting EF compensation into ETH and Ethereum-native stablecoins. The thread frames MEV as the next front in a longer cypherpunk effort and targets privileged orderflow, cartelized builders, and trusted relays as structural failure modes to eliminate.

title: Aztec Reaches L2Beat Stage 2 After Governance Revokes Rollup Contract Ownership
blurb: Aztec, the privacy-focused Ethereum layer-2, reached Stage 2 on L2Beat after an on-chain governance action revoked ownership of its rollup contract, making the code immutable with no admin override and a permanent user exit window. The designation is L2Beat's top decentralization tier, removing the security-council backstop that most rollups retain and leaving Aztec governed entirely by smart contracts.

title: Secret Network's Axelar Bridge Drained $4.67M via Infinite-Mint Flaw
blurb: Secret Network suspended its Axelar bridge after an attacker exploited a minting flaw to drain $4.67M in wrapped tokens over seven undetected days, forging IBC packets through a self-run validator chain that minted unbacked assets against missing escrow checks. The contract bug dated to March 2023 and survived a March 2026 migration, with Secret's default transaction encryption hiding the shortfall until a failed transfer surfaced it.

title: Intercontinental Exchange, OKX expand access to tokenized equities via joint venture co-chaired by former Gov. Cuomo
blurb: Intercontinental Exchange, the NYSE parent, and crypto exchange OKX formed a 50-50 joint venture to give OKX customers in the US and abroad access to ICE futures and NYSE tokenized equities markets. The venture, co-chaired by former New York Governor Andrew Cuomo, will register as a US broker-dealer and FCM, and follows ICE's investment in OKX at a $25B valuation in March.

title: Bank of England Publishes Policy Statement and Draft Rules for Systemic Stablecoins
blurb: The Bank of England published a policy statement and draft Code of Practice for systemic sterling stablecoins, replacing proposed per-user holding caps with a £40B issuance ceiling per coin and a backing-asset mix of up to 70% short-dated UK gilts and at least 30% unremunerated central bank deposits. Issuers can file feedback until September 22, with HM Treasury, not the BoE, designating which coins count as systemic.

title: U.S. Senate passes housing bill that carries four-year ban on a Fed CBDC
blurb: The U.S. Senate passed the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act in an 85-5 vote, attaching a four-year ban on a Federal Reserve central bank digital currency that runs through the end of 2030. The provision is largely symbolic, as the Fed has no active CBDC project and new Chair Kevin Warsh opposes the idea, but it would become binding if the House passes the bill and Trump signs it.

title: JaredFromSubway MEV bot hacked in $15 million crypto theft
blurb: JaredFromSubway, one of Ethereum's most aggressive sandwich-attack MEV bots, lost $15M after an attacker seeded fake pools and tokens that tricked its opportunity-detection logic into granting ERC-20 approvals to attacker-controlled contracts, eventually draining WETH, USDC, and USDT via transferFrom. After a $3M return bounty went unanswered, the operator raised the offer to $7.5M for half the funds plus $1M to the community.

title: Morgan Stanley Files Amended S-1s for Spot Solana and Ethereum Trusts
blurb: Morgan Stanley filed amended S-1s for spot Solana and Ethereum trusts, naming BNY Mellon and Coinbase Custody as joint custodians, a 0.14% sponsor fee, and NYSE Arca tickers MSOL and MSSE. The fee matches its Bitcoin ETF MSBT and undercuts rival altcoin issuers, though no effective date is set and demand for single-asset altcoin wrappers remains unproven.

title: Crypto Heist Fueled by Elaborate Fake Reputation-Boosting Campaign
blurb: Check Point uncovered a clipboard-hijacking campaign that fakes credibility across GitHub, SourceForge, and YouTube to drain Windows and macOS crypto wallets.

title: Why Zelle wants to launch a stablecoin
blurb: Zelle announced Zelle USD, a stablecoin for international payments, just a week after the bank consortium TCH committed to tokenized deposits instead. The split reflects different jobs: stablecoins move dollars across any wallet 24/7 on public chains, while tokenized deposits keep one bank's yield and protections but settle only inside a closed network.

title: CFTC Opens Comment on 24/7 Energy Futures and Perpetual Oil Contracts
blurb: The CFTC issued a request for comment on running standard futures on a 24/7 schedule and listing perpetual contracts tied to physically delivered energy commodities like crude oil. The move signals interest in importing crypto-native perpetual designs into oil and gas derivatives, with comments due within 30 days of Federal Register publication ahead of any formal rulemaking.

title: World Liberty Financial's USD1 Supply Grows 9.7% in a Week to $4.85 Billion
blurb: World Liberty Financial's USD1 stablecoin grew its circulating supply 9.7% in seven days to $4.85 billion, roughly $427 million in new tokens, making it the fourth-largest dollar-pegged stablecoin behind Tether, USDC, and Sky's USDS. The mint coincides with Aster routing real-world-asset perpetual settlement exclusively through USD1, even as competitors Sky's USDS and PayPal's PYUSD contracted over the same window.

title: Franklin Templeton Closes 250 Digital Acquisition, Launches Franklin Crypto for Institutional Allocators
blurb: Franklin Templeton closed its acquisition of 250 Digital and launched Franklin Crypto, an actively managed digital-asset unit targeting pensions, sovereign wealth funds, and other institutional allocators, with part of the consideration paid in its on-chain BENJI tokens. The deal folds CoinFund's former liquid crypto strategies into the $1.78T manager and adds a third institutional product line alongside its BENJI tokenized-treasury fund and pending Bitcoin DRIP ETFs.

title: Chainlink Brings Samsung, Toyota and Sony Pricing On-Chain With APAC Equities Streams
blurb: Chainlink launched APAC Equities Streams, an oracle feed putting live on-chain pricing for large Asia-Pacific listings including Samsung, SK Hynix, Toyota, Sony and SoftBank, starting with Japan and Korea. The feeds let developers build equity perps, prediction markets and structured products keyed to Asian companies during local trading hours, with Mainland China, Hong Kong and Taiwan support listed as coming soon.

title: Five Former EF Researchers Launch Ethlabs, an Independent Non-Profit R&D Lab for Ethereum
blurb: Five former Ethereum Foundation researchers, including proposer-builder-separation lead Ansgar Dietrichs and MEV researcher Barnabé Monnot, launched Ethlabs, an independent non-profit R&D lab backing Ethereum as a universal settlement layer.
